skills.name: Aquatic Skills
skills.description: Build water safety knowledge and swimming skills, from your first splash to advanced scuba diving and underwater navigation in open water.

requirements.competency: I know when to use a PFD (Personal Floatation Device).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe when a PFD is necessary.

requirements.competency: I can float for five seconds and glide for five metres my front and back without assistance.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can enter a swimming pool and (under the supervision of a lifeguard) demonstrate the water skills.

requirements.competency: I can put my face in the water and blow bubbles.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can blow bubbles underwater.

requirements.competency: I understand the importance of the buddy system, and how it works for swimming and water activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate (in practice) the buddy system (selecting a buddy, watching out for their buddy, etc.).

requirements.competency: I know how to stay safe while playing around water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate awareness of risks by using (and encouraging others to use) protection such as sunscreen, UV clothing, hats and sunglasses (where appropriate).

requirements.competency: I can get an object off the bottom in chest-deep water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate this skill in a pool or a lake.

requirements.competency: I know three different types of animals that live in the ocean.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can name three different animals that live in the ocean.

requirements.competency: I can swim with my head in the water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can name or describe the sea animals.

requirements.competency: I can swim 10 metres (any stroke) without assistance.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate this skill under the supervision of a lifeguard.

requirements.competency: I know how to put a PFD by myself.
requirements.requirement: Scouts (under the supervision of a lifeguard) can demonstrate their ability to swim with their heads in the water.

requirements.competency: I know how snorkel gear works.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can correctly put on PFD’s and test them. For purposes of comfort, warmer water (such as a swimming pool) is ideal for this demonstration.

requirements.competency: I have snorkeled in a pool or open water (such as a lake).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how snorkeling equipment (snorkel, snorkel vest, wetsuit, fins) works and when a wetsuit may be necessary.

requirements.competency: I can explain common water safety risks and how to avoid them.
requirements.requirement: Dive shops are able to provide equipment and support this activity with professional instruction if desired.

requirements.competency: I can use a snorkel and adjust my mask to fit comfortably.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain common risks, such as drowning, hypothermia and sunburn. Scouts can explain common solutions for risks such as a buddy system, wearing exposure protection and staying out of the water in cold conditions.

requirements.competency: I know how to remove a cramp in my leg with a buddy’s help.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the “stay on face” test to confirm if a mask fits correctly. Scouts can clear a snorkel partially flooded with water.

requirements.competency: I can put a PFD while in the water and use the HELP and huddle positions.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate cramp removal by pulling the fin tip towards themselves, either on themselves or on a buddy.

requirements.competency: I can swim 25 metres in a pool (using any stroke).
requirements.requirement: Scout can demonstrate the HELP survival position with the legs tucked in and in a huddle.

requirements.competency: I can recognize the signs of a panicked snorkeler or diver and know how to call for help.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate they are capable of moving in the water.

requirements.competency: I have achieved one of the following: Aquaquest Stage 6, YMCA Swimmer Level, Red Cross Swim Kids Stage 5, or I can demonstrate equivalent skills.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify someone wildly flapping his or her arms and gasping for air as somebody who is in distress. Calling for help can be something as simple as yelling to attract attention or calling 911 on a telephone.

requirements.competency: I can free dive with snorkel and mask to 1.5 metres and fetch an item from the bottom, and clear my snorkel upon surfacing—without lifting my head out of the water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can provide proof of external qualification or demonstrate comparable skills to a qualified individual.

requirements.competency: I can explain the hazards of shallow water blackout.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can fetch the object without a time limit.

requirements.competency: I know what gear is necessary for a water-based snorkel Adventure, including protective clothing, masks and sunscreen.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain what shallow water blackout is and the steps that can be taken to prevent it.

requirements.competency: I know how to select a safe place to snorkel.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the equipment and why it is required.

requirements.competency: I have snorkeled in open water and observed at least one marine creature.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe what make a snorkel site safe: it is away from boat traffic, swells, surge, marine hazards, etc.

requirements.competency: I know why ear equalization is necessary when snorkelling or diving at depth.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have been out for at least a half-day snorkel Adventure.

requirements.competency: I have tried an introductory Scuba experience in a pool (Bubblemaker/SEAL Team/Discover Scuba Diving).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe why it is important to know how to equalize.

requirements.competency: I can identify five species in my local aquatic environment (either the surface or underwater), including hazardous species.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can maintain neutral buoyancy and clear a mask of water while underwater. Scouts can open their eyes underwater without goggles.

requirements.competency: I can achieve the “Swim to Survive” standard.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can name five animals, insects or plants that live on or under the water.

requirements.competency: I have completed at least Emergency First Aid, or an equivalent course.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can fall or roll into deep water, tread water for one minute, swim 50 metres—all in one continuous attempt, without touching the bottom or shoreline or side of a pool.

requirements.competency: I have completed the Open Water Diver Certification.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to use a recognized provider for this course.

requirements.competency: I have gone for two additional dives after the Open Water Diver Certification dive.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to use a recognized provider for this certification.

requirements.competency: I have talked with a younger Section about my diving experience.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to dive under the supervision of an experienced adult diver and as per their certification level.

requirements.competency: I have assisted with Scouts (who are at Stage 3 or 4) learning to snorkel in open water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have met with younger non-certified Scouts and spoken to them about my diving experience and how they can become certified.

requirements.competency: I have logged at least five open water dives and assisted in the planning.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to assist in this teaching under direct adult qualified dive instructor supervision.

requirements.competency: I can navigate with a compass underwater and understand the specific challenges of underwater navigation (currents, lack of landmarks, etc.).
requirements.requirement: Diving with an outside organization (dive club, dive shop, etc.) is also acceptable for this requirement.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a marine environmental service project, cleaning up a water body.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to meet this requirement in accordance with their level of dive certification.

requirements.competency: I have two of the following experiences: • I can shoot an underwater photo or video and understand the impacts of water light. • I have either found or placed an underwater geocache. • I have used a dry suit (in cooler waters). • I have performed basic repairs my gear (replacing a mouthpiece with a spare etc.). • I have taken part in a non-penetration wreck dive or any other specialty dive course. • I have helped a younger Scout at Stage 4 or 5 learn how to...(Scout’s Choice).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can achieve this requirement with an outside organization, such as a dive club, dive shop, etc.

requirements.competency: I have completed an Advanced Open Water Certification.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can achieve this requirement with a non-Scouts organization, such as a dive club, dive shop, etc.

requirements.competency: I have led a less experienced buddy a dive through a site that is new to the Scout.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to use a recognized provider for this certification.

requirements.competency: I have drawn a rough map of a dive site.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can achieve this requirement with an outside organization, such as a dive club, dive shop, etc.

requirements.competency: I have assisted Scouts with their dives at Stage 5 or 6.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have shared and explained this map to their Patrols.

requirements.competency: I have organized a dive trip for a Rover Crew.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to assist in this teaching under direct adult qualified dive instructor supervision.

requirements.competency: I have completed a Rescue Diver Certification course, or have learned and mastered the curriculum to demonstrate the equivalent skills.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will be involved in securing rental equipment, checking for restrictions on the dive site and ensuring the dive site’s suitability for the skill stage of all involved.

requirements.competency: I have assisted Scouts at Stage 6 or 7 with learning dive navigation or other advanced dive skills.
requirements.requirement: Scouts demonstrating this skill are to do so under an adult qualified Rescue Diver instructor direct supervision.

requirements.competency: I have completed one of the following dives: • A “deep dive” to more than 80 feet/24.36m • An altitude dive • A dive nitrox • A DPV dive • A search-and-recovery dive
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to assist in this teaching under direct adult qualified dive instructor supervision.

skills.name: Camping Skills
skills.description: Develop the skills to plan, prepare for, and lead camping experiences in all seasons and environments, from your first overnight to multi-day wilderness expeditions.

requirements.competency: I can collect small sticks for a campfire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can search for and return with some dry sticks for starting a campfire. Scouts can separate tinder, lightweight sticks and logs.

requirements.competency: I can follow directions while at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the ability to successfully follow simple instructions.

requirements.competency: I can help pack a bag for camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can assist with the packing of a bag for camp. Scouts can unpack their bags and then re-pack them, explaining what they are doing.

requirements.competency: I can keep my camping gear neat and tidy.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate in a camp setting how to keep things tidy to maintain safety and comfort. Scouts can describe what would happen in adverse weather conditions if their gear was left untidy.

requirements.competency: I can care for my basic personal gear an overnight camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how to check the condition of basic personal gear and show how to care for it. Scouts can explain the impact on their safety if their gear is not working (for example, if the batteries in their flashlight are not fresh).

requirements.competency: I can explain the use of the buddy system at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the buddy system and how and why it is important to use at camp.

requirements.competency: I can describe the different emergency services in the camp area and how to call them.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to call emergency services when an accident takes place on a camp. Scouts can explain the information they will need to provide to the emergency responder.

requirements.competency: I can set out my sleeping area for a good night’s sleep at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to roll out a sleeping bag, pillow and sleeping mat and show that their sleeping area is organized and tidy.

requirements.competency: I have spent one night at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to meet this requirement under direct adult qualified dive instructor supervision and as per the Scout’s dive certification.

requirements.competency: I can explain the importance of following directions at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have attended their first camp (possibly the ‘family camp’ described in BP&P).

requirements.competency: I can help pitch a tent at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain why and how they must listen to instructions, and how to ask for clarification if they don’t understand.

requirements.competency: I behave safely around fires at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can list the items they need to bring with them for an overnight camping event, including clothing and personal care items. • Spare clothing • Wet weather gear • Eating gear • Repair equipment • Wash gear • Sleeping equipment

requirements.competency: I can identify and explain the three elements of the fire triangle.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the value of camping equipment and demonstrate how to go about checking and caring for equipment. Scouts can explain safety implications of poor or dysfunctional camp equipment. Scouts can demonstrate how to keep personal camp equipment in working order. Scouts can show when items of camp equipment are in need of repair. Scouts can describe the quality of different items of camp equipment.

requirements.competency: I have spent two nights in a tent at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe what clothes they need to bring for different weather conditions. • Basic clothing for • Outer shell overnight camp • Wet weather gear • Layer system

requirements.competency: I can list what personal gear to bring an overnight camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can give examples of foods in each food group. Scouts can give examples of what makes a serving from the basic food groups and can show where to locate the required servings for their age group as outlined in the guide. In a group setting, Scouts can use the guide to help plan a nutritious meal.

requirements.competency: I can look after all my personal gear while at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate at camp how to store food safely. Scouts can demonstrate how to properly wash their hands. Scouts can maintain a clean working area while working with different types of food. Scouts can demonstrate how to prevent spoilage when there is no electricity and how to cover and protect foods and surfaces.

requirements.competency: I can explain what clothing to bring an overnight camp.
requirements.requirement: Working in a team (or with an experienced cook) Scouts can demonstrate how to clean and prepare various food items and how to check that food is cooked thoroughly. Scouts can demonstrate how to use knives safely when cutting food items. Scouts can use proper techniques when lifting hot liquids. Scouts can use proper techniques when lifting hot pots and pans.

requirements.competency: I can help explain how to use the Canada’s Food Guide at camp and help to plan a nutritious meal.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can recognize serious injuries and demonstrate how to ask for help. Scouts can demonstrate how to call 911 or their local emergency number and can request assistance from the nearest adult.

requirements.competency: I can describe safe food handling and hygiene at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify the tent, fly, poles and pegs.

requirements.competency: I can help prepare food for cooking at camp and be safe while cooking at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate putting up a tent with other Scouts.

requirements.competency: I can get help if someone is hurt while at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate care and safety around fires. Scouts can state basic fire safety rules at camp: • No horseplay • Keep a safe distance • No poking at the fire • Follow instructions of the person in charge of the fire

requirements.competency: I can identify the main parts of a tent.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain each part of the fire triangle (fuel, heat, oxygen) and demonstrate the role of each element in a good fire.

requirements.competency: I can help others learn about camping.
requirements.requirement: Scouts spend at least two nights camping while completing this stage.

requirements.competency: I can describe how weather can affect our camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts display a willingness to help others learn in a natural way over a period of time, rather than mount a single display of expertise.

requirements.competency: I can discuss the seven principles of Leave No Trace.
requirements.requirement: Scouts show an awareness of the value of camping equipment. Scouts can explain that if equipment is not working properly, it is likely to fail in bad weather conditions. Scouts can demonstrate how to go about checking and caring for equipment. Scouts can explain the safety implications of poor or dysfunctional equipment. Scouts can keep personal equipment in working order. Scouts can demonstrate how to repair items of equipment.

requirements.competency: I can show how to pitch a tent with the help of others.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can present a packed bag for inspection. Scouts can demonstrate and discuss, while unpacking and repacking: • The value of the method used in the packing process • The necessary equipment to pack • Where to place soft items • Where to place heavy items • Where to place food • What is meant by “first in, last out” • What wet weather equipment to bring

requirements.competency: I can make a hot drink a campfire at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can plan a meal using the guidelines of Canada’s Food Guide.

requirements.competency: I can clean up a fire area after camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the proper methods to keep food safe in camp (using food containers and/or coolers as required).

requirements.competency: I have spent seven nights at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can assist in the cooking of a meal in a camp setting.

requirements.competency: I can audit my personal gear for camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use pots safely to prevent tipping. Scouts can use protective equipment or utensils while working with hot items while cooking.

requirements.competency: I can pack a bag for camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can clean a small wound. Scouts can apply a bandage to wound.

requirements.competency: I can help plan a basic balanced meal for camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can access information from the appropriate weather forecasters in their area. This could be from websites, television weather channels or news broadcasts, radio stations or phone apps.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate how to store food at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the different types of weather likely to occur in the area they plan to travel to, given the time of year. Scouts can explain what will happen if it rains in the camp area. Scouts can explain what will happen if it is very hot. Scouts can explain what extra gear they need to bring (just in case).

requirements.competency: I can assist in cooking a meal at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts discuss a basic knowledge of the principles of Leave No Trace and how they affect the way groups camp.

requirements.competency: I can be safe while cooking at camp.
requirements.requirement: Working with a team, Scouts can demonstrate how to properly pitch a tent.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate first aid treatment for a minor cut or scratch at camp, and explain how to prevent infection and describe the signs of infections.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can make a hot drink on an open fire with cooking pots.

requirements.competency: I can get a weather forecast for a camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can clean up the pit, wood pile and area around the fire pit.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate shared teamwork while at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have spent at least four nights camping while completing this stage. Scouts have spent at least two consecutive nights at camp while completing this stage.

requirements.competency: I can assist pitching tent with my team at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can participate in camp as full members of the Patrol. Scouts can play a number of roles while on camp and generally add to the wellbeing of the whole Patrol.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate safe practices around fires and cooking equipment to minimize the risk of burns, scalds and other injuries at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can indicate (in list form) the items of clothing they need to bring with them for various camping activities over a number of days for a standing camp. Scouts can show consideration of proper clothes provision for wet weather. Scouts can explain the benefit of the equipment design as it relates to a standing camp. • Spare clothing • Wet weather gear • Eating gear • Repair equipment • Wash gear • Sleeping equipment

requirements.competency: I have spent 12 nights at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to correctly use any of the individual items of group gear.

requirements.competency: I can list the personal gear for a standing camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to go about checking and caring for equipment, considering: • Safety implications of poor or dysfunctional equipment • Keeping personal equipment in working order • How to repair items of equipment • The quality of different items of equipment

requirements.competency: I can show how to use group gear safely at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to use a tool correctly and are aware of any safety implications. • Be able to use a tool correctly to do the job it was designed for • Display your skill in using a particular tool

requirements.competency: I can show proper use, care and maintenance of group gear during and in between camps.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the type of containers best suited for a camp setting to keep food away from animals. Scouts can demonstrate the proper methods to store containers while in camp. Scouts can demonstrate when and how to use methods such as hanging food containers to prevent animal access. Scouts can demonstrate proper food safely in a camp setting.

requirements.competency: I can use basic camp tools safely.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can clean and treat wounds using appropriate methods for the injury. Scouts can apply an appropriate bandage to a wound to promote proper healing.

requirements.competency: I can store and cook food safely at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can name the seven principles of Leave No Trace and apply each of the principles at camp.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate how to treat cuts and minor burns, and prevent infection at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain what kind of terrain is good for pitching a tent. Examples include: • Level ground • Shady • Rocky ground • Sunny • Near/away/on hill • Close or away from trees

requirements.competency: I can explain and demonstrate the seven principles of Leave No Trace.
requirements.requirement: Working with a team, can demonstrate how to properly pitch a tent.

requirements.competency: I can find the best place to pitch a tent at camp and explain my reasoning.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate a consistent and high level of fire risk management behaviour.

requirements.competency: I have assisted in the organization of two camps for my team or others.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have spent at least four of the twelve nights camping while completing this stage.

requirements.competency: I can describe the weather forecast and record the weather for the duration of camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have actively assisted in the planning of two separate camps for their team or others.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate the appropriate measures for minimizing and dealing with food waste, solid waste and human waste, in keeping with Leave No Trace principles at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can actively assist the person in charge of planning the activities at a camp.

requirements.competency: I can pitch a variety of tents and shelters at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can indicate (in list form) the items of clothing they need to bring with them for various camping activities over a number of days for a lightweight camp. Scouts can explain factors to consider for proper clothes and provision for wet weather. The weight of the pack is also a concern; Scouts can describe weight-saving measures. Scouts can explain the benefits of the chosen equipment design as it relates to lightweight camping. • Spare clothing • Wet weather gear • Eating gear • Repair equipment • Wash gear • Sleeping equipment

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate measures to secure tents for inclement weather at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can list the Patrol equipment necessary for a variety of camps. Scouts can discuss how each item is relevant and what safety equipment is required. With regards to a lightweight camp, Scouts can discuss how the load might be distributed among the party.

requirements.competency: I can select suitable locations for a standing or lightweight camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can show how to properly maintain and care for the tools being used at camp. Scouts know how to store the tools for use at another time.

requirements.competency: I can show best layout for a campsite and explain my reasons.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can mentor other Scouts such that they have successfully completed the packing requirements for Stages 1-4.

requirements.competency: I can light, maintain and use a fire to cook a balanced meal at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can create, with a team, a well-balanced menu plan for a weekend camp.

requirements.competency: I have spent 18 nights at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use at least two different types of cooking fires. Scouts can explain the advantages and disadvantages of different stoves. Scouts can build and use two types of cooking fires to cook a meal. Scouts can properly use two different types of camp stoves.

requirements.competency: I can assist in planning a camp program of activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe heat and cold injuries such as sunburn, frostbite, hypothermia, etc. Scouts can describe proper activity levels and clothing to prevent heat-, cold- and sun-related injuries. Scouts can seek help for any of the above conditions.

requirements.competency: I can show the personal gear needed for an overnight lightweight camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use the weather information they have researched and present it to their Troop Leader and to the Troop in general in a clear, concise fashion that it easy to understand.

requirements.competency: I can explain how the type of camp affects the choice of equipment needed.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the proper way to deal with garbage and camping refuse and how to dispose of it correctly. Scouts can demonstrate the proper method of dealing with kitchen and human waste in a wilderness environment.

requirements.competency: I can use, maintain and store tools safely at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can pitch lightweight tents, standing tents, lean-tos, tarps, etc.

requirements.competency: I can teach another youth what to pack for a camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to set up a tent properly for rainy, snowy or windy conditions—considering tie-down and staking techniques.

requirements.competency: I can plan a balanced menu for camp with a team.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can select a location for the camp based on type (standing/ lightweight/hike-in/etc.).

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate how to use different cooking methods with different fuel types at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how and where camp equipment is set up at a campsite (i.e. kitchen, tents, shelter, chopping area).

requirements.competency: I can prepare for and help prevent heat-, cold- and sun-related injuries at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can prepare a complete meal (not just one menu item).

requirements.competency: I can teach camping skills with my team at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have spent at least six of the 18 nights at camp while completing this stage, including one night of lightweight camping.

requirements.competency: I can teach another youth to prepare a meal to be cooked a fire or improvised stove at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can teach others on their team new camping skills while at a camp.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate treatment of heat-, cold- and sun-related injuries at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can be responsible for the planning and implementation of all aspects (transportation, site location, menu, equipment) of a two-night camp.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate responsibility for myself at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate knowledge of the different types of activity that are possible on the campsite and how best to maximize the opportunities they present. Timetabling and equipment considerations need to be displayed.

requirements.competency: I can recognize weather signs and prepare for their impact camp activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can actively assist the person responsible for organizing transportation for a camp.

requirements.competency: I can travel while following the seven principles of Leave No Trace.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are safety aware and can discuss realistic possible emergency situations. Scouts can explain the type of equipment that is present on the campsite. • First aid kit • Survival bag • Safety ropes • Emergency shelter

requirements.competency: I can describe how to choose the best tent for a specific camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate basic procedures for cleaning and caring for equipment. Scouts can demonstrate how to clean, care for and store tools, tents and cooking equipment.

requirements.competency: I can teach how to pitch a tent at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can prepare a list of personal and group gear for a standing camp, including all required tools, portable shelters (tarps and tents), cooking equipment, emergency equipment, and all other optional or recommended items.

requirements.competency: I can help organize campsite setup and takedown.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can acquire the necessary menu items planned for a team’s weekend camp.

requirements.competency: I can help research proposed camping area and locate services.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the different types of cooking stoves available for camp use (single and double burner, propane and naphtha.) Scouts can explain when each stove type and fuel type is appropriate for the type and season of camp planned.

requirements.competency: I have spent 24 nights at camp in three different seasons, including weeklong camp, whjle completing this stage.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can show other Scouts how to cook meals in a fire using tinfoil or on a created stove (such as a hobo stove design).

requirements.competency: I can plan and lead a weekend camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the causes of hypothermia, hyperthermia, sunburn, frostbite etc. Scouts can identify the signs and symptoms of exposure to the elements. Scouts can treat weather-related injuries and medical conditions.

requirements.competency: I have spent two consecutive nights lightweight camping while completing this stage.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can recognize and take steps to manage themselves in all environmental elements they are exposed to.

requirements.competency: I can plan a program of activities for camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the effects that different types of weather have on the local surroundings. Scouts can recognize the various types of clouds and explain the weather conditions they represent. Scouts can recognize and explain how changing temperatures, wind direction and humidity affect the weather.

requirements.competency: I can assist with the organization of transportation to camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate a consistent behaviour with all Leave No Trace practices.

requirements.competency: I can explain group emergency equipment for a camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can select a tent based on weather, season and location of the camp.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate to others how to care for, store and maintain group gear for camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can assist younger Scouts with pitching tents.

requirements.competency: I can prepare a list of personal and group gear required for a standing camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can assist in leading setting up and taking down camp (i.e. kitchen, tents, picking site, shelter).

requirements.competency: I can help plan a menu and purchase food for a weekend camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can book a camp with all appropriate paperwork (including an emergency plan with directions to the nearest hospital).

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate to others how to use a variety of cooking stoves at camp and explain to others when each type is most effective.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have spent at least six of the 24 nights at camp while completing this stage. Scouts must have camped at least three nights out in every season.

requirements.competency: I can plan and lead a two-night backcountry camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have camped in a remote setting where all the equipment must be transported in a pack or by a self-propelled watercraft.

requirements.competency: I can take responsibility for myself and my team while at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have been responsible for the planning and implementing all aspects (transportation, site location, route, menu, equipment) of a two-night backcountry camp.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate how to plan for and adapt to changing weather patterns at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have shown the ability and knowledge required by planning the activities for both a standing camp and a lightweight camp.

requirements.competency: I can teach the seven principles of Leave No Trace as they apply to a camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate a capability to seek information from and by a number of sources. Scouts can organize a suitable transport to and from the campsite.

requirements.competency: Scouts teach the Leave No Trace principles to Scouts who do not know Leave No Trace.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can actively assist the person responsible for planning an expedition.

requirements.competency: I can organize campsite setup and takedown.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can present a listing of all group emergency equipment, specifically identifying any broken or missing items.

requirements.competency: I have spent 30 nights at camp in all four seasons, including two nights without a Scouter while completing this stage.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have mentored other Scouts such that they have successfully completed the group gear/tools requirements for Stages 4, 5, and 6.

requirements.competency: I can plan activities for at least two different types of camps.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can present the list to their campmates in preparation for a campout.

requirements.competency: I can organize required transportation for camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can teach others how to properly use different types of stoves and fuel types. Scouts can teach the proper use and storage of fuel (i.e. propane and naphtha).

requirements.competency: I have helped plan and lead a backcountry camp of a minimum of two consecutive nights.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the risks various activities may have and are able prepare any required safety measures to reduce the risks.

requirements.competency: I can audit group emergency equipment for camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can recognize and take steps to reduce any perceived issues that may arise in a team environment.

requirements.competency: I can teach another youth how to care for, store and maintain group gear and tools they need for camps at Stages 4, 5, and 6.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can recognize the signs of rainstorms, wind or snowstorms, and know how to protect themselves and their Patrol.

requirements.competency: I can create personal and group gear lists for lightweight camping excursions.
requirements.requirement: Scouts teach the Leave No Trace principles to Scouts who do not know Leave No Trace.

requirements.competency: I can teach the use of various stoves and effective use to another youth at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain to another Scout what types of tents are best for certain seasons and types of weather.

requirements.competency: I can help prepare and describe an emergency plan for expected risks and hazards at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can take a lead role in the setting up and taking down of camp (i.e. kitchen, tents, site selection, shelter). Scouts can appoint others’ direction and duties.

requirements.competency: I have mentored someone else who was responsible for planning and leading a camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have camped at least six of the 30 nights camping while completing this stage. Scouts have camped for at least four nights out in each season.

requirements.competency: I can source, compare and organize transportation options for local and foreign locations.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have mentored another Scout who was successful in completing the Stage 6 requirement to plan a camp.

requirements.competency: I can describe an expedition plan and how the needs of participants have been met in its planning and preparation.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate a capability of seeking information by a number of sources. Scouts can organize the transport to and from the campsite.

requirements.competency: I can prepare for a specialized expedition.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can plan, organize and run a camping expedition so that everyone will find the camp both fun and challenging. • discuss abilities of his/her team • grade hiking terrain and be aware of difficulties that may be encountered • choose a route that is challenging but not dangerous for those taking part • select suitable program activities for a weekend and long term camp

requirements.competency: I can make recommendations to improve group equipment for various camp types.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can plan and organize expeditions that require special skills (e.g. mountaineering, snowshoeing or canoeing), adding whatever skills necessary to their existing skills set.

requirements.competency: I can determine if specialized training is required for camp activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can share with other Scouts improvements that can be made to the existing group gear.

requirements.competency: I can use knowledge of weather patterns to change activities as required at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the need to obtain specific training to safely participate in an activity.

requirements.competency: I have spent 36 nights various types of camps.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can recognize the signs of upcoming weather and adapt plans the group may have to accommodate the weather conditions. This may require changing the venue for the event or changing the activity altogether.

requirements.competency: I can source amenities and local places of interest for various camp types.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have spent at least six of the 36 nights while completing this stage.

requirements.competency: I can budget, prepare and manage every aspect of a camping expedition.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate a capability to seek information by a number of sources. Before the camp, Scouts can research information on the local shops, places to eat, hospital, religious services, etc. that other Scouts can be availed of on camp. Scouts can conduct research into what in the locality of the campsite is of historical, artistic or geographical interest.

requirements.competency: I have acted as expedition leader at least two camping expeditions.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to prepare a budget and manage that budget over a camping activity to achieve a break-even situation. Scouts can run or assist in preparing and managing at least three camping adventures.

requirements.competency: I can plan and execute camping expeditions in all types of locations, regions, and internationally.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have led at least two camping expeditions, which are to be at least four days long.

requirements.competency: I am able to source local training required for the specific camp or activity.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can run, in conjunction with others, successful camps and expeditions no matter the factors impinging on possible success or failure. Scouts can plan at least one camping expedition in a far-away part of Canada or internationally.

requirements.competency: I have spent 42 nights various types of camps.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can locate appropriate training providers to gain the necessary knowledge to safely participate or carry out an activity.

skills.name: Emergency Aid Skills
skills.description: Learn to keep yourself and others safe — from basic first aid and home safety to wilderness emergency response and mass casualty management.

requirements.competency: I have made my own personal first aid kit.
requirements.requirement: Scout’s kit should contain: • a list of emergency numbers • gloves of my size • an emergency blanket • 5–10 adhesive bandages (e.g. Band-Aids™) • pencil and paper

requirements.competency: I use the “buddy system” when outdoors.
requirements.requirement: In a practice drill, Scouts can demonstrate: • checking the scene for dangers (and staying away from them) • checking the person who is ill or injured only if it safe to do so • calling an adult for help or calling 9-1-1 on the telephone • staying with the sick or injured person until help arrives

requirements.competency: I can signal for help if needed when outdoors.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate: • gently cleaning the wound with soap and water • putting pressure on the wound if it is still bleeding • applying an adhesive bandage that covers the wound

requirements.competency: I always tell an adult where I am going.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain: • drinking water during activities or when it is warm outside • wearing proper clothing for weather conditions • using sunscreen when playing out in the sun

requirements.competency: I carry a whistle and visible signal covering when I go out in the bush.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know not to talk to or go anywhere with strangers.

requirements.competency: I know to “hug a tree” if lost, or stay in one place if there are no trees.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can recite their own address and home phone number.

requirements.competency: I know how to be safe around a campfire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain why it is not safe to play with matches and lighters.

requirements.competency: I know how to behave around wildlife.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can list a few items in the home that may be dangerous (for example, hot or sharp) to touch, such as stoves, barbecues, fireplaces, knives, tools, etc.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate the basic approach to first aid.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can recite the ‘911’ telephone number (if applicable in their community) and state the type of emergencies required for calling the number: police, fire, ambulance. Scouts can make a simulated call to describe the help needed to a 911 operator.

requirements.competency: I can treat minor cuts or scrapes.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know to buddy-up with a friend during outdoor activities.

requirements.competency: I can be responsible for my own health.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know how to make themselves seen and heard for searchers.

requirements.competency: I can be responsible for my own safety.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain why they need to tell an adult in charge where they are going if leaving the adults with small groups or a buddy during an outdoor activity.

requirements.competency: I know my address and location in an emergency.
requirements.requirement: Scouts carry a whistle and visible signal covering (e.g. bright orange garbage bag) with them when in an outdoor setting where they could become lost.

requirements.competency: I know not to play with matches and lighters.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate in a practice drill the technique of staying close to a tree and staying in one spot when lost. Scouts can describe how they know that they are lost. Scouts know to yell and whistle for help. Scouts know to stay warm and dry if they can. Scouts know to spread out visible items for searchers to see from the air. Scouts know to eat food and drink water if they have these items.

requirements.competency: I know how to spot things in my home that are not safe.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can state that are to stay a safe distance away from fire. Scouts can state that they will not run and play in the area of the fire. Scouts can state that they will not touch the fire without the help of a Scouter. Scouts can state that they will not touch tools, such as an axe or saw, without proper training and supervision. Scouts can state that they will not throw anything into the fire.

requirements.competency: I know the different emergency services that are available, and how and when to call them and what to say.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have spent at least six nights camping while completing this stage.

requirements.competency: I have added items to my own personal first aid kit and I know how and when to use it.
requirements.requirement: Scouts show a respect for wildlife and do not do anything to harm animals or their habitat.

requirements.competency: I can direct a responder to a location where help is needed.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have added the following items to their first aid kits (made in Stage 1) and should be able to describe basic use of the materials in first aid situations: • 5–10 gauze pads • 1 roll of medical tape • triangular bandages • roller gauzes

requirements.competency: I know the hazards of water sources in my local area.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe what could be done by in some of the following emergency situations: house fire, earthquake, tornado, flooding, power outage, etc.

requirements.competency: I wear my helmet when using my bicycle, skateboard or scooter.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe and demonstrate minor burn care: cooling the burn with clean, cool water for at least 10 minutes, or until the burn is cool.

requirements.competency: I can dress myself appropriately for the weather.
requirements.requirement: Scouts, with help of their families, have made a home escape plan that could include the following: • fall & crawl in fire and smoke • two exits from every room • get out and stay out • a safe family meeting spot outside the home • what to do if a stranger is at the door

requirements.competency: I know some of the wild animals in my area and how to be safe around them.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can verify where the emergency numbers are posted in a convenient location in their homes.

requirements.competency: I can find a safe place in bad weather.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to stop, drop and roll.

requirements.competency: I can keep myself safe in emergency situations.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify the four hazard symbol pictures (explosive, corrosive, flammable, poison) on a number of household products.

requirements.competency: I can care for someone who has a minor burn.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe how they should look for potential hazards.

requirements.competency: I have made a home escape plan with my family.
requirements.requirement: Scouts demonstrate the ability to follow Scouters’ directions to keep safe, and can explain why it is important to follow directions from an adult.

requirements.competency: I know where the list of emergency telephone numbers is located in my home and I know how to call them.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can lead or direct responders to the specific location where help is needed. A practice drill can be used to show this skill.

requirements.competency: I know how to stop, drop and roll if my clothes are fire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the dangers of getting too close to creeks, streams, rivers, lakes and other bodies of water in all seasons. Scouts know to have a responsible adult present when swimming and to never swim or play in water alone.

requirements.competency: I can recognize a warning label a product and know to leave the product alone.
requirements.requirement: Scouts consistently wear a properly fitted helmet when riding bicycle, skateboard or scooter

requirements.competency: I can check for dangers at an emergency situation.
requirements.requirement: Scouts consistently demonstrate wearing the appropriate clothing for all weather conditions.

requirements.competency: I always follow directions from a Scouter.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know what wildlife could be dangerous in the area and how best to react when they come in to contact with these animals.

requirements.competency: I can do first aid for large wounds.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the best place to go in case of a thunder and lightning storm, rain, snow, cold, heat, and/or severe wind.

requirements.competency: I have made a list of emergency numbers (such as police, fire, ambulance and poison control) and posted it by a telephone in my home.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate proper care for large wounds: • applying pressure to the wound with a clean dressing (such as gauze) • securing the dressing with a bandage such as roller gauze or a triangular bandage • demonstrate the use of the Scout neckerchief as a triangular bandage • if the bleeding does not stop, applying more dressing and bandages and seeking additional help

requirements.competency: I know how to help create an escape plan for a building or activity location in case of fire.
requirements.requirement: Scout can demonstrate proper care for a common nosebleed including: • pinching the nose • tilting the head forward • keeping the nose pinched for at least 10 minutes • seeking help if the bleeding does not stop after 10 minutes of pressure

requirements.competency: I know the “Rules of the Road” for safe bicycling.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate proper care for a choking victim according to the latest methods taught by a first aid organization.

requirements.competency: I can identify some of the hazardous plants in my area, and those I may encounter when travelling in Canada.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the signs and symptoms of shock: • pale, cool, sweaty skin • fast and shallow breathing • light-headedness • increased heart rate • confusion Scout can demonstrate the proper care for shock: • putting the person in a comfortable position • keeping their body temperature normal (a blanket for someone who is cold, or a shady place for someone who is warm) • getting help and keeping the person calm

requirements.competency: I know some of the international distress signals and when to use them.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the proper care, including: • talking calmly with the person • keeping the person comfortable • treating for shock • reassuring the person that help is on the way • staying with the person unless they need to go somewhere to call for more help

requirements.competency: I can treat bee stings and reactions to some local plants, such as stinging nettle.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to lock, unlock and secure windows, doors and entryways in their homes.

requirements.competency: I can perform first aid for nosebleeds.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the dangers of playing near train areas.

requirements.competency: I can perform first aid for someone who is choking.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the hazards of such activities.

requirements.competency: I know the signs and symptoms of shock and how to treat shock.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the dangers of playing near listed dangerous areas.

requirements.competency: I can comfort someone who is ill or injured.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can verify the emergency numbers are posted in a convenience location in their homes.

requirements.competency: I can show how to lock, unlock and secure all windows, doors and other entryways into my home.
requirements.requirement: Scouts (working with friends) can make and practise a fire escape plan for a classroom, meeting hall or similar location.

requirements.competency: I know the dangers of playing or near train tracks, trestles, crossings and train yards.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain and demonstrate safe bicycle riding.

requirements.competency: I know the dangers of touching power lines with a stick or ladder; climbing electrical power poles, towers and substations; and poking electrical outlets.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify in nature or describe some poisonous/hazardous plants local to their area (e.g. poison ivy, poison sumac) and know their hazards (e.g causes rash when touched, vomiting when ingesting berries) and can demonstrate how to avoid their toxic elements.

requirements.competency: I know the dangers of playing around storm sewers, construction sites, garbage dumps or dumpsters, ice-covered water or water areas, dams, vacant buildings, farm machinery, quarries, old wells and unfriendly animals.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate two or three international distress signals for a variety of situations (such as lost on land or on water).

requirements.competency: I can place someone into the recovery (safe airway) position.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the treatment for a bee sting and exposure to poisonous plants.

requirements.competency: I can care for my feet while outdoors.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate placing someone in the recovery position, including care for an unconscious person.

requirements.competency: I can explain how to prevent and treat heat and cold injuries.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain: • not giving the person anything to eat or drink • finding out what the poison was • calling the local Poison Control number or 9-1-1

requirements.competency: I know how to treat and report (if appropriate) insect and animal bites.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain: • not rubbing the eye • blinking fast • if there is still something in the eye, flushing the eye under running water (with the affected eye towards the ground) • getting medical assistance quickly if material remains in the eye

requirements.competency: I can provide care for someone who is poisoned.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the items in the kit to their Patrols.

requirements.competency: I know the first aid treatment for dirt in an eye.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can check that: • paint, paper, rags and flammables are away from heat • hot water tank is set below 54°C (130°F) to help prevent scalding • stove-top pot handles are turned away from the front of the stove

requirements.competency: I know what goes into a home first aid kit.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate with a smoke alarm.

requirements.competency: I can help reduce the risk of fire and burns in the home.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain what to do if: • the lights go out • a fuse blows or circuit breaker trips • a water pipe bursts • they smell natural gas • a drain backs up • a fire or carbon monoxide alarm goes off

requirements.competency: I can show how to test and care for a smoke alarm.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can report the local community emergency shelter information to their Patrols.

requirements.competency: I can manage a home emergency situation.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can take out the group first aid kit before an outing and review the contents with the outing group.

requirements.competency: I know where my local community emergency shelter is located or how to find out where a community emergency shelter is located if one is needed.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know how to; • detect a pre-blister hot spot on a foot • treat a blister • identify and prevent trench foot • select the proper footwear for the activity • keep toe nails clipped before hiking • hike in dry socks and footwear

requirements.competency: I know what is in our group first aid kit and know how to use the kit.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the heat and cold injuries encountered when outdoors and the behaviour, equipment, medical and shelter plans to avoid these.

requirements.competency: I have successfully completed an Emergency First Aid and CPR (Level A) course from a recognized provider.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain what types of insect and animal bites could happen when outdoors, what the first aid treatment should be and what the follow-up plan should be (if needed).

requirements.competency: I can correctly record everything that has happened at the scene of the accident.
requirements.requirement: Recognized providers include: • the Canadian Red Cross Society • St. John Ambulance • the Lifesaving Society • the Heart and Stroke Foundation of Canada • Canadian Ski Patrol

requirements.competency: I know the rules and why they are important for a home pool, community pool or a body of water used for swimming.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate (in a practice drill) a written record of: • who was ill/injured • what happened • when did this happen • what treatment was done • where did this happen • what additional help did you get

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate how to safely use and care for a barbecue.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the pool rules.

requirements.competency: I have assisted in providing training to others in aspects of emergency aid.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate, by cooking a barbecue meal for their Patrol mates, how to safely use and care for the appliance.

requirements.competency: I know how to deal with an incident, injury or illness in a remote outdoor location and how to summon help.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are able to assist with emergency aid training at a Scout meeting or camp.

requirements.competency: I know how and when to use flares, mirrors, horns and other long-distance signalling devices.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how to: • secure the site and individual(s) from further hazard and harm • care for the victim(s) and rest of the group • make and activate a plan to raise an alarm for assistance • begin an evacuation or establish a treatment site

requirements.competency: I know how to build a stretcher from improvised materials.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain these devices, and the advantages and disadvantages of each.

requirements.competency: I can use a compass or a GPS device to find direction and travel to a desired location.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can build a stretcher from items brought along on a typical outdoor activity day.

requirements.competency: I have participated in and successfully completed a Standard First Aid with CPR (Level C) course or a Marine Basic First Aid with CPR (Level C), from a recognized provider.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can lead a navigational exercise in the field that includes the following: • Scouts can read a map and locate themselves on a map • Scouts can navigate to any fixed point on a map and do so with a safe and effective route plan • Scouts can establish an evacuation route on a map

requirements.competency: I have acted as an Emergency Response/ Preparedness resource at least one Scouting activity.
requirements.requirement: Recognized providers include: • the Canadian Red Cross Society • St. John Ambulance • the Lifesaving Society • the Heart and Stroke Foundation of Canada • Canadian Ski Patrol

requirements.competency: I can explain the different classes of fires, and how to use different types of fire extinguishers.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can participate as a resource on campout, jamboree or any other event where a large group or Patrol is present.

requirements.competency: I have met with a member of a community-based emergency response team and discussed his or her role and responsibilities in my community (e.g. search and rescue, police, fire, ambulance or coast guard).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the fire classes and fire extinguisher type to a Patrol.

requirements.competency: I can identify common poisonous plants in my area and I know how to treat exposure and symptoms.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can arrange for a community-based emergency response team member to visit a Scout meeting or for a Patrol to travel and meet with the response team member.

requirements.competency: I have acted as a member of a first aid team at least one outdoor activity.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate their poisonous plant knowledge to a group of Scouts.

requirements.competency: I have successfully completed an outdoor curriculum first aid course.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can be a designated first aid provider on a Scout outing for a minimum of four days. This service does not have to be at one outing.

requirements.competency: I know what specialized equipment is required in my field first aid kit based upon my activities, skill level and certification, and I know how to use and care for the equipment.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can attend one of the following: • Advanced Wilderness & Remote First Aid course (Canadian Red Cross) • Wilderness First Aid Level III course (St. John Ambulance) • Wilderness First Responder — from commercial vendor • or equivalent certification level from a recognized provider

requirements.competency: I respond to emergency situations and follow best practices for first aid, as per whatever first aid certification I hold.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can maintain their first aid skill and certification competence by: • attending a renewal course • participating in first aid scenarios • being a first aider at events • reviewing course manuals

requirements.competency: I have prepared and maintain a 72-hour home emergency kit.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should create this emergency kit with their families.

requirements.competency: I have filled out Scouts Canada’s Outdoor Activity application for at least three Scout group events.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to ensure they consider participants, including Scouters, are: • in the Right Place, • with the Right People • at the Right Time, • and with the Right Equipment

requirements.competency: I have acted as a first aider at least four occasions during single-day group outings or two standard weekend camps.
requirements.requirement: Scouts duties include preparing the first aid kit, treating any injuries and properly following-up on any incidents.

requirements.competency: I have acted as an emergency preparedness and management support for at least one weekend standing camp or two Area events.
requirements.requirement: Scouts in this role are to be under the direct supervision of an adult Scouter.

requirements.competency: I can use can use a variety of communication devices effectively in an emergency situation. I have participated in a session correct use of radio communications and protocols (ARES).
requirements.requirement: Scouts know how to use a variety of emergency communication devices, such as: • satellite phone, • Spot device, • InReach device, • Personal Locator Beacon, • VHF/UHF/CB radio, • marine radio, • Emergency Position Indicator Radio Beacon (EPIRB).

requirements.competency: I have met with a member of a community-based search and rescue emergency response team and discussed his or her role and responsibilities in my community.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have arranged for this response team member to meet with a Scout Group.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a wilderness search and rescue operation (training or real).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can spend at the minimum of one days’ time in a SAR operation.

requirements.competency: I have successfully completed an advanced first aid course.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can present their kit to their Patrol for review.

requirements.competency: I know what equipment needs to be in a first aid kit for an activity of at least one weekend in length in the wilderness.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can choose from the following courses: • First Responder with CPR Level HCP (Canadian Red Cross Society), • Advanced Medical First Responder with CPR Level HCP (St. John Ambulance), • Marine Advanced First Aid (as recognized by Transport Canada), • Advanced First Aid (as recognized by the Province of Alberta), or equivalent nationally-recognized certificate, or higher qualification or • hold a current certificate from a recognised body in Emergency/ Disaster Response such as VERC, TeenCERT, Ontario Volunteer Emergency Response Team

requirements.competency: I have been the responsible first aider for an outdoor expedition of at least three nights.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can take a course in the following: • Aquatic Lifesaving and Lifeguarding • Swiftwater Rescue • High Angle Rescue • Boat Rescue • Ice Safety, Glacier/Avalanche Safety • Search and Rescue • Canadian Ski Patrol Training • SCUBA Rescue • TeenCERT Train-the-Trainer, or • Emergency Management Ontario’s BEM-100 [Basic Emergency Management Certificate] or local provincial equivalent or • A training or qualification that can be approved by my Section Leadership Team as meeting this non-first aid certification course requirement.

requirements.competency: I can purify water in a safe manner.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can assess and manage risk in various and constantly changing situations. Scouts can constantly assess hazardous situations as they arise and take measures to limit risk.

requirements.competency: I have built an emergency shelter in the wilderness with minimal equipment, and I have slept in it overnight.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain a circle check on a vehicle. Scouts can instruct younger Scouts (16 years and older) on how to jump-start a car and change a tire.

requirements.competency: I can lead a team at least 100m over wilderness terrain in transporting a patient with an injury (who cannot walk by his or her own power).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can follow the manufacturer’s instructions for the safe start and maintenance of an emergency generator.

requirements.competency: I know the limitation in a wilderness setting when calling for medical evacuation transport.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can lead a session for younger Scouts or another group on the contents and use of a 72-hr home preparedness kit.

requirements.competency: I know what preparations should be made when calling a medical helicopter.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can practise this skill in an incident scenario.

requirements.competency: I can describe and demonstrate proper use of fire extinguishers (or other tools or methods for extinguishing fires).
requirements.requirement: Scouts will arrange for this emergency response search team member to meet a Scout Group.

requirements.competency: I have successfully completed a non-first aid certification course in an area of my personal interest within Emergency Aid.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will have the plan and strategies approved by a Scouter or Group Commissioner.

requirements.competency: As part of taking a non-first aid certification course, I can improve my risk management skills.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate the kit to their Patrols.

requirements.competency: I can safely perform basic emergency repairs an automobile, such as changing a flat tire or jump-starting a car.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to have the appropriate first aid certification for this outing.

requirements.competency: I can start and maintain a consumer emergency generator.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can purify water from a natural source.

requirements.competency: I have taught a group of people the importance of, and what should be in, a 72-hour home preparedness kit.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can build a shelter with whatever they can carry in a backpack; the shelter is to be precipitation-proof.

requirements.competency: I know and can describe the steps required to triage in a mass casualty incident (MCI).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can complete this task as part of an outdoor rescue scenario.

requirements.competency: I have met with a member of underwater community-based emergency response search team and discussed his or her role and responsibilities in my community.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how different locations and terrain require different means of transport.

requirements.competency: I can create a trip plan with detailed risk management strategies for an activity with my group.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the landing requirements, landing site safety and victim packaging requirements.

requirements.competency: I have successfully completed instructor training in the area of my personal interest within Emergency Aid.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can speak about extinguish cooking fires, grassfire, electrical fires, etc.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a multi-casualty emergency exercise.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can choose instructor training in any of the flowing: • First Aid • Aquatic Lifesaving and Lifeguarding • Swiftwater Rescue • High Angle Rescue, • Boat Rescue • Ice Safety, Glacier/Avalanche Safety • Search and Rescue • Canadian Ski Patrol Training • SCUBA Rescue • TeenCERT Train-the-Trainer • Emergency Management Ontario’s BEM-100 [Basic Emergency Management Certificate] or local provincial equivalent or • Any training or qualification approved by the Section Leadership Team as meeting the instructor training requirement.

requirements.competency: I know the health risks, and possible ways to mitigate the risks, when travelling to a part of the world I have not before visited.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can teach an emergency skill based upon the qualifications permitted by instructor’s certificate.

requirements.competency: I have used my instructor qualification to teach Scouts or another community group the course’s curriculum, as permitted by my instructor certificate.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate this competency to the Section Leadership Team by either: • presenting a case study of a situation the Scout has been in that was complicated and in the Scout’s personal interest in Emergency Aid. or • participating in scenarios relevant to the Scout’s personal interest in Emergency Aid that are complicated in nature and include an personal and group evaluation component.

requirements.competency: I can provide immediate treatment and deal with complicated emergency situations.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can perform this service at a Scout or community event approved by the Section Leadership Team.

requirements.competency: I have completed a minimum of 75 hours of volunteer first aid service in addition to those hours already used to complete an earlier stage.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can participate in the planning and implementation of an Emergency Response Plan for a large Scouting event that is five days in length or has over 400 participates for a shorter period.

requirements.competency: I have participated in the preparation and implementation of an Emergency Response Plan for an Area event lasting 5 days or involving at least 400 participants.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can provide instructions and assistance with Scouts working on Stage 7 or 8 of Emergency Skills.

requirements.competency: I have provided Emergency Skills mentorship to a Stage 7 or Stage 8 Emergency Skills Scout.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can arrange for a response team member to meet with a Scout group.

requirements.competency: I have met with a member of community-based emergency air search response team and discussed his or her role and responsibilities in my community.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can help younger Scouts assemble their own survival kits.

requirements.competency: I can assemble, display and describe winter and summer survival kits, and explain how to use them.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can teach a lost in the woods lesson to a younger group.

requirements.competency: I can explain to another group (for example, Wood Badge participants) what to do if lost in the wilderness.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can contact the local Search and Rescue groups to arrange for this participation. Emergency Management exercises are required by Provincial Emergency Management Acts.

skills.name: Paddling Skills
skills.description: Explore Canada's waterways by canoe or kayak, building paddling technique, trip planning, and rescue skills from calm lakes to backcountry rivers.

requirements.competency: I can jump into chest-deep water with my Personal Flotation Device (PFD) on.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate they are comfortable getting into and out of the water with a PFD on.

requirements.competency: I know how to contact the emergency services.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate that they are comfortable putting their faces in the water for 10 seconds.

requirements.competency: I can get in and out of my watercraft safely.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can show that they know and understand the purpose of a PFD; a PFD must be worn at all times when paddling and a PFD is to be put on before entering any watercraft.

requirements.competency: I have taken part in a short paddling adventure of at least one hour.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to properly put on a PFD, ensuring all buckles and zippers are properly fastened and that their PFD is snug enough not to slide over their heads.

requirements.competency: I can blow bubbles in the water for 10 seconds.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can tell the difference between the front and the back of a watercraft and know the proper names for the front and the back.

requirements.competency: I can explain what a PFD is for.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can show the proper use of a paddle with the top hand properly on the grip and the bottom hand on the shaft. Scouts’ hands should be as far apart as their shoulders.

requirements.competency: I can put my PFD and know how it should fit.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate (while in a watercraft) that they must keep their weight low in the watercraft and balance themselves by holding both gunwales. Scouts can demonstrate that if they are not paddling they should sit on the floor and that there is no jumping, sudden movements or horseplay while in the watercraft.

requirements.competency: I can show where the bow and stern are in a canoe or kayak.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know that a poorly cared for PFD is at risk of mould, tears and deterioration. They should also know that a damaged PFD will not work reliably.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate the correct way to hold my paddle.
requirements.requirement: A Scout can explain that cold water means your body will not function as well as it should.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated how to behave safely in my canoe or kayak.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know how to call 9-1-1 or their local emergency number and to request assistance from the nearest adult.

requirements.competency: I can explain why I should care for my PFD.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate that they can get in and out of their watercraft safely.

requirements.competency: I can explain the risks of cold water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain what governmental and non-governmental sources can be accessed as part of the health and safety planning for an international trip.

requirements.competency: I can swim 25 metres with my PFD on.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will have experienced a short paddling adventure. Scouts only need only be in the watercraft. If Scouts want to paddle, they may, but Scouters or more-experienced Scouts are in charge of the watercraft.

requirements.competency: I am familiar with common whistle signals and when they would be used.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate that they are comfortable in the water and able to swim a short distance with a PFD on. Any swimming stroke is acceptable.

requirements.competency: I have used a throw bag.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain that a PFD may not hold a person’s face out of the water if the person is unconscious and that a life jacket will turn a person face up.

requirements.competency: I can identify the parts of my watercraft and my paddle.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain where they are allowed to paddle, as they have been instructed by the person in charge of the paddling activity.
