requirements.competency: I am familiar with the signs and symptoms of mild hypothermia.
requirements.requirement: Scout understand that lake and river water may not be safe to drink due to bacteria, chemicals, parasites and germs that may be present in the water.

requirements.competency: I have taken part in an at least two paddling activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify the five essential pieces of safety equipment and have a rudimentary idea of how to use them. • PFD • Paddle • Signaling Device (whistle) • Bailer • Floating Rope Scouts can explain the use of a flashlight for fog or nighttime paddling.

requirements.competency: I can explain the difference between a PFD and a life jacket.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain basic safety rules for common hazards including: slippery rocks, sharp objects, unstable banks, moving water, etc.

requirements.competency: Before I launch my watercraft, I can show where I am allowed to go canoeing or kayaking.
requirements.requirement: Scouts need to know that when their watercraft capsizes they should to the following: • stay with your watercraft • make noise to get attention • count to five and take a breath • hang onto your paddle if you can • follow the instructions of your rescuer

requirements.competency: I can explain why I should not drink the water from the lake or river I am paddling until it has been filtered or treated.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should have an awareness of the fact that paddling can have a negative impact on the environment and the need to be respectful of the places where we paddle to minimize our impact.

requirements.competency: I can identify the equipment Transport Canada requires me to have in my canoe or kayak.
requirements.requirement: If Scouts see someone in difficulty on the water, they know to call for help by whistling and yelling. Scouts can use a throwing assist if one is available.

requirements.competency: I can explain the safety rules for being near water.
requirements.requirement: While there are variations to exactly how whistles are used, Scouts should be aware of some of the basic whistle signals. • one blast—stop paddling and pay attention for further instructions • two blasts—raft up. If the group is spread out, the lead paddlers should go back and raft up with the back paddlers • three blasts—EMERGENCY—go to who blew the whistle and stand by. Follow the instructions of the person directing the rescue.

requirements.competency: I can list the appropriate action I should take if I capsize in a canoe or kayak.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate an ability to use a throw bag. Distance and accurately at this stage are not the prime objectives.

requirements.competency: I have explained some of the ways that paddling a canoe or kayak can have a negative impact the environment where I am paddling.
requirements.requirement: Any type of self-propelled watercraft and paddle are suitable. Parts should include: • Paddle: blade, tip, neck, shaft, handle, etc. • Watercraft: hull, bow, stern, bum rest, etc.

requirements.competency: I can get help if I see somebody in difficulty the water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can list the signs and symptoms of mild hypothermia: • constant shivering • cold or pale skin • tiredness • fast breathing • low energy (hyperventilation)

requirements.competency: I can swim 100 metres with my PFD using any stroke.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will have completed two paddling adventures of at least one hour in duration each, and have practised their paddling skills with the support of a more experienced Scout or Scouter.

requirements.competency: I have helped a Stage 1 paddler learn to put his or her PFD.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate that they are comfortable in the water and able to swim a short distance with a PFD on. Any swimming stroke is acceptable.

requirements.competency: I have taken part in two paddling activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can discuss options for sizing and understand that the paddle that ‘feels right’ is usually best.

requirements.competency: I know how to choose a paddle that is the correct size.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can paddle their watercraft forward in a reasonably straight line for a short distance. As much as possible, Scouts achieve this skill with another youth with similar skills. Adults or more senior youth may be the passenger.

requirements.competency: With help from my team or my Scouter, I can paddle my canoe or kayak forward a short way.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can rock a watercraft and have it dump. Scouts do not jump out of the watercraft; they attempt to stay with the watercraft.

requirements.competency: I have capsized a canoe while sitting in it.
requirements.requirement: Someone else rights the swamped watercraft and assists the paddlers to get back into their watercraft.

requirements.competency: I can get back into my canoe or kayak with help from someone in another watercraft if my watercraft capsizes.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the ability to get accurate weather forecasts from the internet, radio or television and can discuss what the forecast might mean for their paddling trip.

requirements.competency: I know how and where to get the latest weather forecast for the area where I will be paddling.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how different weather conditions (wind, rain, sun and cold) can impact a paddling trip and have an understanding of the risks associated with different weather.

requirements.competency: I know what the risks are for paddling in different weather conditions.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to make a distress signal such as three whistle blasts or by lighting three fires or putting three watercrafts in a triangle.

requirements.competency: I can make a recognized distress signal.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can throw a throw bag at least 5 metres and be within the length of their watercraft of the target.

requirements.competency: I can throw a throw bag.
requirements.requirement: Stage 3 Scouts can help a less experienced Scout to properly select and put on a PFD.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate the HELP and huddle positions while in the water wearing a PFD.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have completed two paddling adventures of two hours or more each. Scouts must carry out most of the paddling with a Scouter or a more experienced Scout (either in the same watercraft or close by to help if assistance is required).

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate basic canoe strokes (forward, reverse, draw, pry, stop, j, sweep).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the ability to preserve body heat in the event they are stranded in the water for extended periods. They can demonstrate the ability to maintain both positions for a period of five minutes each.

requirements.competency: I can explain why my watercraft needs a painter and can attach one so that it is secure and readily available when I need it.
requirements.requirement: (This competency will vary depending on the region of the country and the season Scouts are paddling in.) Scouts can demonstrate an understanding of the clothing options available and some of the pros and cons of the different options, including: different footwear, different headwear, long-sleeves vs short-sleeves, trousers vs shorts, rain gear and wet suits.

requirements.competency: I can describe water and weather conditions that make paddling unsafe and can explain what to do if I encounter them while I am the water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate an ability to assist in the launching of a canoe from the shore and from a dock. When doing this, they can demonstrate the following: • one person holds the watercraft stable while the other is moving • use paddle to stabilize the watercraft while moving or sitting in position • avoid damage to watercrafts • watercraft should be in the water before the paddler gets into it • stepping into the water is encouraged

requirements.competency: I can explain why canoes and kayaks are important to Canadian aboriginal people and the history of Canada.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate the following technique: • Stern paddler moves ahead of rear thwart and crunches up in a ball • Bow paddler steps over stern paddler to back seat • Stern paddler moves to bow seat • Only one person moving at a time • Communication between paddlers must happen

requirements.competency: I can identify three methods for helping someone in the water to reach safety and have demonstrated how to use them. (This can be demonstrated in open water or a swimming pool.)
requirements.requirement: A Stage 4 Scout will have spent time helping a Scout working at Stage 2 to learn basic safety rules for being near the water.

requirements.competency: I can throw a throw bag so that someone in the water can reach it.
requirements.requirement: See LNT program website: www.leavenotrace.ca/principles

requirements.competency: I have taken part in and logged at least four paddling activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have participated in a raft-up with three or more watercraft in the raft. When rafting-up, watercraft should be touching each other with paddlers holding each other or each other’s watercraft. Scouts understand that the raft-up is used for communication or breaks.

requirements.competency: I can explain what a float plan is.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use whatever paddle strokes they wish to keep the line reasonably straight for 50 metres. Scouts can demonstrate this skill with another Stage 4 paddler, or with a Scouter or more experienced paddler.

requirements.competency: I have participated in at least one paddling activity of at least eight hours’ duration that includes making and eating a meal.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate a basic understanding of what to do to make the watercraft turn in the direction they intend it to go.

requirements.competency: I can explain what clothing should be worn while canoe tripping.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate these different strokes and have a basic understanding of what happens to the watercraft when different strokes are applied.

requirements.competency: I can assist in launching and landing a canoe or kayak.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know that a painter is the rope attached to the bow and/or stern of the watercraft. They can secure the painter to their watercraft using an appropriate knot.

requirements.competency: I can trade places with my paddling partner while the water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts understand water and weather conditions (such as waves, thunderstorms and tides) and can explain the appropriate course of action when they encounter them while on the water.

requirements.competency: I have helped a Stage 2 paddler to explain the basic safety rules for being near water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate (through a group discussion with their patrol, a presentation, skit, story, etc.) an understanding of the importance of canoes and kayaks to Canadians both before and after European contact.

requirements.competency: I can explain the seven principles of Leave No Trace.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify three methods for helping someone in distress in the water, such as: talking someone to safety, throwing something buoyant to the person in distress, or going to the person. Any aids identified should be items that would normally be found on a canoe trip and can include, but are not limited to the following: • throw bags • throwing assists • reaching assist (paddle, pole) • PFD

requirements.competency: I have taken part in a canoe or kayak raft-up and can explain its uses.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the ability to throw a throw bag to someone in the water so that the person can easily reach it within a few swimming strokes.

requirements.competency: By myself or with a paddling partner, I can paddle a canoe or kayak in a straight line going forward for at least 50 metres.
requirements.requirement: Scouts maintain paddling logs. The log include date, location, distance, time, participants and something interesting about each activity.

requirements.competency: I can make my canoe or kayak turn in the direction I want it to turn.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know that a float plan documents: where they are going, when they will return, where the closest aid is and who is in the paddling group.

requirements.competency: I have participated in at least two two-day paddling trips.
requirements.requirement: During this paddling activity, Scouts are tasked with all the paddling. Older paddlers can be in the watercraft or in a nearby watercraft to help if required. Scouts who have completed the Paddle Canada Waterfront program have demonstrated most of the paddling and safety requirements for Stages 1–4. Items specifically related to tripping may not be part of the Waterfront program.

requirements.competency: I can explain Scouts Canada’s guidelines for paddle sports.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have participated in a minimum of two paddling trips that include overnight camping.

requirements.competency: I can assist Stage 3 paddlers to get back into their swamped watercraft.
requirements.requirement: With the help of a more experienced Scout or Scouter, Scouts can assist in the completion of a trip emergency plan (as outlined on pages 10–11 in the Field Guide to Canadian Scouting). Scouts can complete their own float plan that includes their intended route, dates, potential camping locations and intended return date. Scouts can complete an emergency plan that identifies potential hazards they may encounter, a system for dealing with hazards and a method for contacting emergency services if required.

requirements.competency: I can help paddlers at Stage 1 identify the parts of their paddles and their canoes or kayaks.
requirements.requirement: As a part of their Patrol, Scouts can contribute meal ideas for one each of breakfast, lunch and supper for a menu.

requirements.competency: I have completed and logged at least six days of backcountry paddling.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know the signs and symptoms and recommended treatment of weather-related injuries such as: hypothermia, hyperthermia, heat exhaustion, sun stroke, frost bite, etc.

requirements.competency: I have attained at least the Paddle Canada Canoe Basic Skills level of paddling certification.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can swim a capsized watercraft to shore or paddle it (10 m) while sitting in it.

requirements.competency: I have helped prepare an emergency plan and a float plan with a more experienced paddler for a two-day paddling trip.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can properly pack their personal gear so that it will stay dry during a paddling trip. They have considered options such as ziploc bags and garbage bags or commercial dry bags and barrels, and can discuss the pros and cons of these options.

requirements.competency: I have participated in creating a menu for an overnight trip.
requirements.requirement: In a swimming pool or in open water, Scouts can demonstrate the ability to complete a canoe-over-canoe rescue.

requirements.competency: I can recognize the symptoms of weather-related injuries and know how to treat them.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the ability to light a fire quickly and efficiently.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate a self-rescue with my canoe or kayak.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can make a personal survival kit (including signaling devices, first aid items, food items, tinder and a fire starter) that is waterproof and buoyant.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate how to pack my personal gear so that it will stay dry.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have reviewed and can explain Scouts Canada’s Safety Procedures with particular attention to the Adventure and Swimming Standards.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a canoe-over-canoe rescue as both the rescuer and the one being rescued.
requirements.requirement: Using the watercraft-over-watercraft rescue technique, Scouts can assist Stage 3 paddlers to get back into their watercraft.

requirements.competency: I can light a fire using no more than three matches.
requirements.requirement: Scouts working at Stage 5 can teach paddlers at Stage 1 to identify the parts of their paddles and their canoes or kayaks.

requirements.competency: I have made a personal survival kit as identified in the Field Guide for Canadian Scouting (pages 27–28).
requirements.requirement: Scouts have logged six days of backcountry paddling. This can include a mix of day trips and overnight trips.

requirements.competency: I can load my canoe with personal and group gear for a multi-day trip.
requirements.requirement: Contact Paddle Canada to determine this requirement.

requirements.competency: I know the limits of weather conditions that are safe to paddle in.
requirements.requirement: In loading a canoe, Scouts consider weight distribution, wind direction and ease of access for items that might be needed while on the water (e.g. throw bag, rain gear).

requirements.competency: I have completed and logged at least eight days of backcountry canoe tripping.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate a variety of ways to purify water, such as filtration, chemical purification and boiling.

requirements.competency: I have taught at least one paddling skill, one paddling safety element and one paddling knowledge item to paddlers working Stages 1–4.
requirements.requirement: Contact Paddle Canada or other paddling certifying body to determine this requirement. Examples of provincial bodies include: ORCKA ( the Ontario Recreational Canoe and Kayak Assoc.), Paddle Alberta, Alberta Recreational Canoe Association, Recreational Canoeing Association. of BC, Canoe Kayak BC, Paddle Manitoba, Manitoba Recreational Canoeing Association, Canoe Kayak New Brunswick, Canoe New Brunswick, Paddle Newfoundland and Labrador, Newfoundland Canoe Association, NWT Canoeing Association, Canoe Kayak Nova Scotia, Canoe Nova Scotia, Canoe Kayak Saskatchewan, Saskatchewan Canoe Association, Yukon Canoe and Kayak Club, Manitoba Paddling Association, Canoe Kayak PEI, PEI Recreational Canoeing Association, Canoe Kayak Ontario, Association Quebecoise de canoe-kayak de vitesse, Federation Quebecoise du Canot Camping, etc.

requirements.competency: I have explained the seven principles of Leave No Trace to a Stage 4 paddler.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can create a gear list for the personal and group gear required on a four-day trip, considering the size and nature of the group, the season and the destination.

requirements.competency: I have kept a journal of my canoe excursions that includes both details of the trip and my personal reflections.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the importance of tent locations (including slope of land/drainage), safe entry and exit to water, absence of dead branches overhead/standing trees, suitable and appropriate location for cat hole for human waste, etc.

requirements.competency: With my paddling team, I can plan a healthy menu for our canoe adventure and can help prepare the meals we have planned.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain that regulations can be found by consulting websites, park offices and local land managers.

requirements.competency: I can explain how I can access emergency assistance while in the backcountry.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain what waste needs to be packed out and what waste can be disposed of in the backcountry, and how the former can be responsibly disposed-of.

requirements.competency: I have completed at least one paddling trip that is four days, 50 km, and has a minimum of three different campsites and includes portages.
requirements.requirement: At any time during a paddle trip, Scouts can establish on a map where they have traveled from, where they are and where they are going to.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated several methods for ensuring that water is safe to drink.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know that a few elements to consider are: sky and cloud conditions, change in wind direction or speed and humidity.

requirements.competency: I have attained at least Paddle Canada Lake Canoe Skills Introduction Tandem certification (or provincial equivalent where applicable).
requirements.requirement: Scouts know that they need to consider wind, waves, tides, current and precipitation before and during a paddling outing.

requirements.competency: I can create a gear list for the personal and group gear required a four-day trip.
requirements.requirement: From Stage 6 onwards, tripping will normally take place in backcountry areas that are: not accessible by car, have limited communication options (no cell coverage) and require two hours or more to reach assistance if it is needed.

requirements.competency: I can explain the features of a good campsite a waterway.
requirements.requirement: This can be accomplished over time and does not have to happen at one outing.

requirements.competency: I know how to find out the backcountry camping regulations in the area where I will be travelling.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should know the principles without having to refer to written notes.

requirements.competency: I can explain how to deal with waste while traveling in the backcountry, including greywater, solid waste, food scraps and human waste.
requirements.requirement: Readings from the journal should be shared with the Patrol.

requirements.competency: I can read and understand a topographical map, and can use it and a compass to tell where I am and where I am going while a canoe trip.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can plan a healthy menu for a canoe adventure, considering the proper amount of calories, food allergies and preferences.

requirements.competency: I can recognize conditions that may precede bad weather.
requirements.requirement: Communication devices include: SPOT (GPS emergency communicator), satellite phone, radios, InReach, PLB and cell phone where the service is reliable.

requirements.competency: I can efficiently and safely carry my canoe and my gear across a portage of a least 700 metres.
requirements.requirement: This trip can be with or not with a Scout group.

requirements.competency: I can use several different methods for cooking.
requirements.requirement: Can be accomplished as a tandem carry with another member of the team.

requirements.competency: I have taught at least one paddling skill, one safety element and one knowledge item to paddlers working Stage 4 or Stage 5.
requirements.requirement: Evidence of this certification is to be presented to confirm completion of this Stage.

requirements.competency: I have participated in 12 days of canoe tripping at Stage 7 and recorded the information in my trip log.
requirements.requirement: Evidence of this certification is to be presented to confirm completion of this Stage.

requirements.competency: I have completed a canoe trip of six days’ duration, 100 kilometres in distance. The trip included four or more campsites, and any number of portages or swifts of moving water (Class 1 or Class 2).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate this practice.

requirements.competency: With my team, I have developed a hazard assessment and risk control plan detailing how we will deal with injuries, illness, loss of equipment and other potential emergencies our trip.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain these benefits to a Patrol.

requirements.competency: With my team, I have developed a float plan for my canoe trip.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe how to outfit a canoe for safety and comfort, listing features including: kneeling pads, painters, yoke, tie downs, air bags and spray skirts.

requirements.competency: I know what items should be in a first aid kit for backcountry travel as per Transport Canada regulations.
requirements.requirement: This technology includes: satellite phone, GPS and SPOT communicator.

requirements.competency: I have attained a minimum of standard first aid training from an accredited agency. Standard wilderness first aid is strongly recommended.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate this skill, considering trim for wind direction and current.

requirements.competency: With my team, I can plan all the details for an overnight canoe trip and have evaluated the trip afterwards to ensure that our planning was complete and appropriate.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can assist with creating a menu and cooking a meal.

requirements.competency: I have attained a minimum of Paddle Canada Lake Canoe Skills Intermediate Tandem level certification.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate several different methods for cooking, including: fire, gas stove, Dutch oven, solar and reflector oven.

requirements.competency: I have attained a minimum of Paddle Canada Moving Water Canoe Skills Introduction Tandem certification.
requirements.requirement: Not required to be taught all at one outing.

requirements.competency: I always wear an approved paddling helmet when I paddle in rapids.
requirements.requirement: Log book should be available as evidence of completed requirement.

requirements.competency: I can explain the relative benefits of various types of canoe materials and designs.
requirements.requirement: Log book should be available as evidence of completed requirement.

requirements.competency: I know how to outfit a canoe for safety and comfort.
requirements.requirement: Plan should be in written form and presented to the Patrol.

requirements.competency: I know how to use basic tripping technology and understand the pros and cons of the devices I use.
requirements.requirement: Plan should be in written form and presented to the Patrol.

requirements.competency: I can show how and why the way I load my canoe will be different for different water conditions.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to go through the first aid kit and explain the items and the use of each.

requirements.competency: I understand the food requirements for canoe trippers and have prepared a nutritious and delicious menu for a multiday canoe trip.
requirements.requirement: Any national or provincial first aid certification body is appropriate.

requirements.competency: I can plan and carry out a backcountry canoe trip with my team of at least 14 days, 250 km and a minimum of 11 different campsites.
requirements.requirement: Planning details should include: • knowing the area to be paddled in • preparing a menu and arranging for the food and supplies • preparing a float plan and emergency plan • ensuring all required permissions have been attained • organizing gear and equipment Post trip evaluation should examine what went right, what went wrong, and how to improve for the next trip.

requirements.competency: I have successfully completed an advanced wilderness first aid course of at least 40 hours duration (80 hours preferred) with a certified accreditation agency (St. John Ambulance, Red Cross or equivalent).
requirements.requirement: The trip will include portages and Class 2 moving water. Scouts need to complete their moving water course (as per Paddling 8.4) and an advanced wilderness first aid course (as per Paddling 8.10) before this extended trip.

requirements.competency: I have assisted in teaching a paddling course to Stage 5 or Stage 6 paddlers.
requirements.requirement: The inspection of rapids should be conducted from the shoreline wherever possible.

requirements.competency: I can inspect a rapid to determine the best lines for running it.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can recognize and describe a rapid that is beyond the limits of their own skills.

requirements.competency: I can recognize a rapid that is beyond my skill level to run.
requirements.requirement: Contact Paddle Canada to arrange for this certification.

requirements.competency: I have attained Paddle Canada Moving Water Canoe Skills Intermediate Tandem certifications.
requirements.requirement: Log book should be available as evidence of completed requirement.

requirements.competency: I have completed and logged at least 25 days of canoe tripping at Stage 8.
requirements.requirement: Contact Paddle Canada to arrange for this certification.

requirements.competency: I have successfully completed a Paddle Canada Waterfront Canoeing Instructor course.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must to able to identify the responsibilities of a trip leader including: • responsible for safety of the trip • ultimate maker of safety and navigation decisions • identifies the lead and sweep watercraft • aware of the potential risks and has the tools to address them (risk management)

requirements.competency: While my extended trip, I have been the designated trip leader for at least one day (Two Scouts—but not more than two—may share the trip leader role).
requirements.requirement: Scouts must properly prioritize the rescue—people, watercraft, gear. Practice rescues should be completed in less than perfect conditions (eg. in rapids, in windy conditions, in cold water).

requirements.competency: I can rescue a capsized loaded canoe and get the swimmers to safety.
requirements.requirement: See requirement 7 Stage 8.

requirements.competency: I can explain the responsibilities of a trip leader.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to complete a wilderness first aid course prior to their extended trip.

requirements.competency: I have completed instructor level certification in at least one Paddle Canada discipline. • tandem or solo lake paddling • tandem or solo moving water paddling • canoe tripping
requirements.requirement: Scouts will work under the direction of an adult who is qualified to lead the course.

requirements.competency: I have completed and logged at least 25 days of backcountry canoe tripping, which may include time spent leading trips for less experienced paddlers.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are qualified to teach introductory or intermediate level skills courses in the chosen instructor level certification.

requirements.competency: I know and follow the Transport Canada requirements for guided canoe trips.
requirements.requirement: Log book should be available as evidence of completed requirement.

requirements.competency: I have completed all necessary paperwork for leading a trip, including a float plan, an emergency plan and Scouts Canada requirements.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must be aware of federal legislation governing human-powered watercraft, including the requirements for guided trips (primarily SOR 2010-91 part 3). Under the definitions of the legislation, all trips with a designated leader are considered to be ‘guided’. Refer to www.laws-lois.justice. gc.ca/eng/regulations/SOR-2010-91/page-13.html#docCont

requirements.competency: I have successfully completed a swift water rescue course.
requirements.requirement: Plan should be in written form and shared with the Patrol.

requirements.competency: I can lead a group of Stage 6 or Stage 7 paddlers a multi-day excursion.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have successfully completed a swift water rescue course. Scouts should complete this course prior to leading any trip that includes moving water.

requirements.competency: I have taught at least two sanctioned Paddle Canada courses to Scouts working at Stage 5–8.
requirements.requirement: Stage 9 Scouts will assist younger paddlers to plan for their trip and will accompany them during the paddle trip. The Stage 9 Scout will ensure that the trip is properly equipped and has a healthy menu and the provisions to prepare it. Scouts will help the younger trippers to create an emergency plan (complete with an evacuation process and communication systems) and will ensure that trip participants have the skills to do the trip safely.

skills.name: Sailing Skills
skills.description: Learn to sail confidently from your first time on the water through racing, navigation, and earning instructor-level certification on the open water.

requirements.competency: I can explain the risks of cold water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should simply understand that cold water means your body will not function as well as it should.

requirements.competency: I have taken part in a short sailing adventure of at least an hour.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will show that they know and understand the purpose of a PFD. A PFD must be worn all times when sailing, and a PFD is to be put on before entering any watercraft.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a fun physical fitness program designed for sailing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate that they know how to properly put on a PFD, making sure all buckles and zippers are properly fastened and will make sure that their PFD is snug enough not to slide over their heads.

requirements.competency: I can explain what a Personal Flotation Device (PFD) is for.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can show how to avoid sunburns by using sunscreen, and wearing a hat to avoid sunburn and heatstroke.

requirements.competency: I can put my PFD and know how it should fit.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can show how to contact the emergency services at a local emergency number, and to request assistance from the nearest adult.

requirements.competency: I can show how to avoid sunburns.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will be able to tell the difference between the front and back of their boat and will know the proper names.

requirements.competency: I can show how to contact emergency services.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will be able to demonstrate that they are comfortable getting into and out of the water wearing their PFD’s.

requirements.competency: I can show where the bow and stern are in a sailboat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate that they are comfortable putting their faces in the water for ten seconds.

requirements.competency: I can jump into chest-deep water wearing my PFD.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate, while in a boat, that they must keep their weight low in the boat and are able to balance themselves by holding the gunwales. They should also demonstrate that if they are not sailing that they should sit on the floor and that there should not be any jumping, sudden movements or horseplay while in the boat.

requirements.competency: I can blow bubbles in the water for ten seconds.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will have experienced a short non-powered sailing adventure. Scouts only need be in the watercraft and must wear their PFD properly.

requirements.competency: I can explain and have demonstrated how to behave safely in my sailboat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must have the appropriate paddling certification(s) to teach these courses.

requirements.competency: I can explain the safety rules for being near water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should participate in a fun physical fitness program designed for sailing that improves flexibility, builds stamina, improves strength, quickens their speed, and improves their skill level, suitable to the age of the participant.

requirements.competency: I can lift a boat with help from others, rig a sailboat, and practise getting into and out of my boat safely.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to explain the safety rules for being near water including wearing a PFD, having a buddy and telling an adult that they are going near the water.

requirements.competency: Before I launch my boat, I can show where I am allowed to go sailing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to describe the early signs and symptoms of hypothermia, and know that the body loses heat 25 times faster than in air. • constant shivering • cold or pale skin • tiredness • fast breathing (hyperventilation) • low energy

requirements.competency: I can swim 50 metres wearing my PFD.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should know that a poorly cared-for PFD is at risk of mold, tears and/or deterioration. They should also know that a damaged PFD will not work reliably.

requirements.competency: I can sail away from dock, hold the tiller, pull in the mainsheet, ease the sheets, and sail straight for one minute.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to explain that a PFD may not hold a person’s face out of the water if they are unconscious, and that a life jacket will turn a person face up.

requirements.competency: I have taken part in an at least two daysails of four hours each or four daysails of two hours each.
requirements.requirement: While there may be variations to exactly how whistles are used, Scouts should be aware of some of the basic whistle signals. • One blast means ease the sheets to depower the sails and listen for further instructions; • Two blasts means tack/gybe and sail towards the leaders’ safety boat or the designated fleet leader in another sailboat; and • Three blasts means EMERGENCY—go to who blew the whistle and stand by to assist if possible.

requirements.competency: I am familiar with the signs and symptoms of mild hypothermia.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should know five actions in the event of capsizing. • Stay with the boat. • Make noise to get attention. • Count to five and take a breath. • Hang onto the sailboat. • Follow the instructions of the rescuer.

requirements.competency: I can explain how I should care for my PFD.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to tie the reef knot, sheet bend and figure-eight.

requirements.competency: I can explain the difference between a PFD and a life jacket.
requirements.requirement: Scouts need to be able to identify the twelve key parts of a sailboat. • hull • flotation air bags • mast • dagger board • sprit pole • centerboard • boom • painter • rudder • halyard • tiller • mainsheet

requirements.competency: I am familiar with the three common whistle signals and when they would be used.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should demonstrate an ability to use a throw bag, but without a standard for how far or how accurately it goes at this stage.

requirements.competency: I can describe five appropriate actions I should take if I capsize in a sailboat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate how to rig a sailboat while ashore, then demonstrate how to get into and out of their sailboat safely, how to dry tack, hold the tiller and hold the mainsheet while in shallow water.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate how to tie reef knot, sheet bend and figure-eight.
requirements.requirement: Scouts need to know, and be able to explain, where they are allowed to sail as they have been instructed by the person in charge of the sailing activity.

requirements.competency: I can identify twelve key parts of my sailboat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to demonstrate that they are comfortable in the water and be able to swim a short distance wearing a PFD. Any swimming stroke is acceptable.

requirements.competency: I have used a throw bag.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to practise sailing a boat when on the water with a buddy.

requirements.competency: I can get help if I see somebody in difficulty the water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should have an opportunity to practise their sailing skills.

requirements.competency: I can tack, gybe, sit gunwale, hike, slow down, speed up, bail the boat and balance the boat.
requirements.requirement: If Scouts see someone in difficulty on the water, they should know to call for help by whistling and yelling.

requirements.competency: I can de-rig a sailboat, dry the sails and store all the parts properly.
requirements.requirement: Scouts need to understand that untreated lake, river or ocean water may not be safe to drink due to bacteria, germs, parasites or chemicals that may be present in the water.

requirements.competency: I have taken part in an at least three daysails of four hours each, or six daysails of two hours each.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be aware of the Sail Canada basic hand signals. • one hand in air—stop, look at the leader and listen awaiting further instructions; • two hands overhead with hands on head—come to the safety boat; • thumbs up on one or two hands held high—I am OK signal; and • two arms waving in a vertical arc overhead—EMERGENCY—I need help.

requirements.competency: I know how and where to get the latest weather forecast for the area where I will be sailing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to identify the five essential pieces of safety equipment and have a rudimentary idea of how to use them. • One life jacket or PFD for each person on board • One signalling device (whistle) • One paddle • One bailer • One buoyant heaving line of at least 15 m in length See Transport Canada—Sail boating guide for details on all classes of boats.

requirements.competency: While fully clothed and with a properly fitted PFD, I can tread water for five minutes, then swim 100 metres using any stroke.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should know that sailing can have an impact on the environment and are respectful of the places in which they sail.

requirements.competency: I can explain why I should not drink the water from the lake, river or ocean which I am sailing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to identify or draw the rigging of six types of sailcraft. • cat-rigged • sail board • gaff-rigged dinghy • keelboat • sloop-rigged dinghy • catamaran

requirements.competency: I am familiar with common hand signals and know when they are to be used.
requirements.requirement: Scouts need to know and explain how different weather conditions (wind, rain, sun, and cold) can impact a daysail.

requirements.competency: I can identify the equipment Transport Canada requires me to have in my sailboat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to tie four knots within thirty seconds each. • reef knot • figure-eight • sheet bend • bowline

requirements.competency: I can explain what impact I have the environment while sailing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate that they can rig a sailboat, and get into and out of their sailboat safely on the water from the shore or dock.

requirements.competency: I can identify six types of sailcraft.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to practise sailing a boat when on the water by themselves or with a buddy.

requirements.competency: I can identify the signs of dangerous weather and water conditions.
requirements.requirement: Scouts, by themselves, are able to put all the parts of a boat in proper storage when they are finished with each sailing experience.

requirements.competency: I can tie the reef knot, sheet bend, figure-eight and bowline used by Scouts when sailing or when camping.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should have an opportunity to practise their sailing skills.

requirements.competency: I can rig my boat and then practise getting into and out of my boat safely.
requirements.requirement: Scouts need to demonstrate the ability to get accurate weather forecasts from the internet, radio, marine radio or television, and be able to discuss what the forecast might mean for their daysail.

requirements.competency: I know how to find an appropriate PFD that is the right size and fit for me.
requirements.requirement: To prepare Scouts for a simulated common accident around water, Scouts must demonstrate that they have the skills to self-rescue within 100 metres of shore, while fully clothed and wearing a properly fitted PFD, by treading water for 5 minutes, then swim a distance of 100 metres to shore. Any swimming stroke is acceptable.

requirements.competency: Under the direction of the Skipper, I have sailed my sailboat forward for 200 metres.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to describe what is required to find and put on a properly-sized PFD.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate how to steer a sailboat going upwind or downwind.
requirements.requirement: To ensure the safety of Scouts on the water, Scouts Canada watercraft regulations require that all persons in a boat must wear a properly fitted PFD.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate how to balance my sailboat and can sail the boat flat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts need to be fully able to identify and treat severe hypothermia, and understand the importance of avoiding hypothermia.

requirements.competency: I have capsized the sailboat and recovered to an upright position.
requirements.requirement: Scouts need to be aware of local hazards in and around boats, on the water, in known local conditions and in unknown conditions when near water.

requirements.competency: I can get back into the sailboat solo, or with help from the Skipper, if my boat capsizes.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to describe where to secure a towline and how to prepare a sailboat for towing.

requirements.competency: I can help my Skipper to return the sailboat to the dock or to the beach safely.
requirements.requirement: Scouts need to know and explain how different weather conditions (wind, rain, sun and cold) can impact a daysail and have an understanding of the hazards associated with different weather.

requirements.competency: I can de-rig a sailboat, dry the sails and store all the parts properly.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to describe how to move body position to balance the sailboat and to keep the sailboat flat.

requirements.competency: I have taken part in at least four daysails of four hours each, or eight daysails of at least two hours each, safe, familiar waters.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to describe how to properly trim the sails: where the sails should be when close hauled, and the mechanics of luffing the sail to depower.

requirements.competency: I can toss a throw bag so that someone in the water can reach it.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must be able to tie the eight knots listed below (time limit: 30 seconds for each knot). • reef knot • round turn and two half hitches • sheet bend • clove hitch • figure-eight • bowline • rolling hitch • fisherman’s bend

requirements.competency: I can swim and demonstrate the HELP and huddle positions while in the water wearing a PFD.
requirements.requirement: Under direction of the Skipper, Scouts should be able to sail their boat forward in a reasonably straight line for a short distance. As much as possible, Scouts should do this with another youth. Adults may be a passenger and/or crew.

requirements.competency: I am aware that everyone must wear properly fitted PFD’s while in a boat.
requirements.requirement: Under direction of the Skipper, Scouts will be able to head up or bear off the wind depending on the wind conditions and direction.

requirements.competency: I have been introduced to self-help procedures and can explain how to perform the HELP position by myself and the huddle position with others.
requirements.requirement: Under direction of the Skipper, Scouts should be able to move body position to balance the sailboat and to keep the sailboat flat.

requirements.competency: I am familiar with the signs and symptoms of severe hypothermia.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will have rocked a sailboat until it capsizes to gain a sense of the critical “heeling point of no return”. Scouts should never jump out of the boat, and they must stay with the boat and help the Skipper recover the boat to an upright position.

requirements.competency: I can describe and demonstrate safety in and the water.
requirements.requirement: A Scout should be able to right a capsized sailboat with the help of the crew/Skipper, or solo, and demonstrate re-entry over the transom or gunwale.

requirements.competency: I know how to properly secure a towline to my sailboat.
requirements.requirement: A Scout, acting as crew, should be able to assist the Skipper to dock or beach the boat safely, and then lift the boat from the water to dry land storage.

requirements.competency: I know the hazards for sailing in different weather conditions.
requirements.requirement: Scouts, by themselves, should be able to put all the parts of a boat into proper storage when they are finished with each sailing experience.

requirements.competency: I understand balance in the sailboat and know how to sail the boat flat.
requirements.requirement: In addition to the daysail completed for the previous three stages, Scouts must have completed at least four four-hour daysail adventures or eight two-hour sailing adventures for a total of 16 hours. Scouts must demonstrate all of the sailing skills expected of a crew with a more experienced Stage 7 Scout/Venturer/Rover Scout in the sailboat.

requirements.competency: I understand how to trim my sail to get the best performance from the sailboat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to toss throw a throw bag to within 2 metres of a person in the water.

requirements.competency: I can tie eight knots that are useful when sailing, canoeing or camping.
requirements.requirement: While in the water, Scouts should demonstrate the solo HELP position and the huddle positions with other Scouts for 7 minutes.

requirements.competency: I know when and how to follow the Sail Coach’s commands.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to describe when to perform the HELP and huddle positions and what the self-help positions are intended to do in the water.

requirements.competency: I can explain the rules established to avoid collision.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must be aware of the Sail Coach’s instructions at all times for their personal safety and for making the Learn to Sail session a positive learning experience.

requirements.competency: I can inspect a rigged sailboat and identify faulty boat parts.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to identify and treat mild symptoms of hyperthermia.

requirements.competency: I can launch a boat from the dock or from the shore.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must demonstrate using reaching assists in open water using: • throw bags • reaching assists—towel, paddle, pole • throwing assist—PFD, life ring • items that would normally be found in a sailboat

requirements.competency: I can trade places with my sailing crew while the water in winds less than 9 knots.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should create a float plan and leave it with their parents or their Scouter as the safety contact at home. The content of the float plan should include: • where they are going • where the closest aid is • when they will return • how to contact them • who is coming with them

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated steering a sailboat heading upwind, or bearing off and going downwind, depending wind conditions and direction.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should know that a painter is the rope attached to the boat to secure bow or stern to dock, mooring buoy or tow boat.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate a self-rescue with my sailboat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should know three active games to play to limber up and do warm-up exercises before launching their boats.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated proper Man Over Board (MOB) procedures while the water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to explain what clothing should be worn while sailing. • footwear • hat • long vs. short-sleeved shirt • long pants vs. shorts • all-weather gear • wetsuit and/or drysuit Clothing will vary depending on the season of the sailing excursion. Clothing may vary between different Scout Councils across Canada.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated making a sail raft and can explain its uses.
requirements.requirement: Level 5 Scouts MUST identify the five pieces of safety equipment required by the Transport Canada and demonstrate their proper use. • One life jacket or PFD for each person on board • One signalling device (whistle) • One buoyant heaving line of at least 15 metres in length • One paddle • One bailer See the Transport Canada’s Safe Boating Guide for details on all classes of boats.

requirements.competency: I can work as part of a team to sail in a straight line going forward for at least 200 metres.
requirements.requirement: A Stage 5 Scout must be able to identify the following: • lateral port and starboard buoys and day beacons • four cardinal buoys • special purpose (diving, keep out, mooring, swimming) buoys • mandatory lights required on sailing vessels underway • mandatory lights when anchored

requirements.competency: I can effectively steer the sailboat while sailing flat, identify wind direction while sailing, make the boat turn and head up or bear off within one boat length of the mark.
requirements.requirement: A Stage 5 Scout must be able to understand explain the following consistent with the Collision Regulations Rule 12–17: • Rule 12 Sailing vessels: opposite tack, same tack, windward tack, leeward tack, downwind tack, tack/gybing • Rule 13 Sailing vessels Overtaking Boat must keep clear • Rule 14 Sailing vessels Head–on situation • Rule 15 Vessels Crossing Situation • Rule 16 Action by the Give-Way vessel • Rule 17 Action by the Stand-on vessel

requirements.competency: I can identify mild symptoms of hyperthermia.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to identify the parts of a boat that would make it unsafe to go sailing if in need of repair. • frayed whippings on lines • torn stitching on the sails • absent telltales on the shrouds • weakened swedges on the forestay and shrouds • loose turnbuckles on the shrouds • weakened or loose holding fittings in place • missing or unusable retaining pin on the pintle of the rudder • missing painter at the bow of the boat

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated how to trim the sail while sailing at all points of sail, including adjusting the sails for wind shifts, and adjusting the sails for puffs or lulls.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should have the knowledge and demonstrate how to launch from the dock or from the shore. • To avoid boat damage, the sailboat should be in the water before the sailor gets into it. • One person holds the boat stable, while the other person slides over the opposite gunwale and into the boat. • When launching from the shore, stepping into the water is encouraged.

requirements.competency: I can manoeuvre the sailboat properly while giving the proper commands to my crew.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to demonstrate exchanging places on the water when sailing in light wind conditions so that two youth on board can experience both helm and jib control without having to go back to the shore or the dock.

requirements.competency: I can safely dock or beach a sailboat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to manoeuvre the boat. • Steer a boat to go upwind and downwind. • Tack and gybe the sailboat under control.

requirements.competency: I can lift the boat from the water to dry storage, de-rig the boat, and store the sails and foils correctly in the boat storage.
requirements.requirement: A Scout in command of a sailboat must be able to right a capsized sailing dinghy and re-enter the sailboat by both themselves and with crew.

requirements.competency: I have participated in one daysail of at least six hours’ duration which includes sailing to and landing at a beach, making and eating a meal, and returning safely.
requirements.requirement: A Scout must be able to sail solo to tack/gybe the boat around, sail back to the location of the man overboard, bring the boat into irons next to the person in the water and help the MOB get back into the boat.

requirements.competency: I have taken part in at least five daysails of six hours each, or ten daysails of three hours each, safe, familiar waters.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should know how to raft boats together while holding the neighbouring boat, keeping hands 30 centimetres above the gunwales to keep fingers clear of being pinched between the hulls: • with two or more boats in the raft • for communication between boats or with a sail coach • for taking breaks on the water

requirements.competency: I have helped a Stage 2 or Stage 3 sailor explain the basic safety rules for being near water.
requirements.requirement: A Scout, in command of a sailboat, can sail with a crew as a team to sail the boat properly.

requirements.competency: I can identify three reaching assists that could be used to help someone in the water to reach safety and have demonstrated how to use one of them.
requirements.requirement: A Scout, in command of the sailboat, must be able to read the wind in order to turn the boat upwind or downwind, and set the sails properly while underway.

requirements.competency: I know how to create and use a float plan.
requirements.requirement: Scouts, in command of the sailboat, must be able to read the wind at all points of sail in order to trim the sail to gain the best performance in changing wind conditions.

requirements.competency: I can explain why my boat needs a painter.
requirements.requirement: Scouts, in command of the sailboat, must be able to issue the proper commands in the correct sequence to safely tack/gybe correctly, set a new course and to stop the boat to the leeward of the coach boat.

requirements.competency: I know games youth can play to promote flexibility prior to going sailing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts, in command of the boat, should be able to: • judge the boat’s speed during its approach before turning head to wind towards the dock and stop the boat within one arm’s length of the dock, allowing the crew to reach out and shinny onto the dock • turn the boat head to wind parallel to the shore in waist deep water, slowing the boat to a stop before both Skipper and crew slide over the opposite gunwales and into the water • stop the boat before hitting the dock or hitting the sand and rocks preventing hull damage

requirements.competency: I can explain what clothing should be worn while sailing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to safely remove the sailboat from the water for proper storage.

requirements.competency: As the Skipper of my sailcraft, I can identify the equipment Transport Canada requires to be the sailboat and demonstrate its proper use.
requirements.requirement: Scouts and crew should tie their daypacks under the forward deck, then sail the boat to the lunch spot.

requirements.competency: I can describe the basic Transport Canada navigational aids the water.
requirements.requirement: In addition to the daysails completed for the previous four stages, Scouts must demonstrate all of the sailing skills expected of a Skipper to ensure the safety of the boat and crew, while sailing in familiar water and winds up to 12 knots.

requirements.competency: I can identify signs of moderate levels of hyperthermia.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be familiar with and capable of sharing their sailing knowledge.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a local club race.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to recognize and identify and treat moderate symptoms of hyperthermia.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a sailing rescue as both the rescuer and the one being rescued and I have experienced a simulated capsize to acquire the knowledge to recover the boat properly.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should know why they should keep their body hydrated.

requirements.competency: I can sail a single / double-handed boat to CANSail 3 skills standards.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • draw air flow around a cat and sloop-rigged sail plan • describe how the presence of the jib accelerates air around the main

requirements.competency: I have participated in at least six daysail outings.

requirements.competency: I have maintained a logbook of my training.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to understand and describe the intent of the local racing rules declared annually.

requirements.competency: I have assisted the sail coach in delivering one safety element, one knowledge item and one on-the-water skill to sailors working Stage 2, 3 or 4.

requirements.competency: I understand and have taken the appropriate actions to maintain hydration.
requirements.requirement: Scouts learning to sail faster should be able to perform these tasks on demand, executing a gybing exercise that includes six gybes in one-minute intervals.

requirements.competency: I understand, and can explain, air dynamics a sail.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • adjust the sail controls to achieve maximum power • depower using sail controls when suitable • steer the sailboat for maximum speed • manage power achieved through proper sail trim

requirements.competency: I can evaluate local sailing hazards.

requirements.competency: I have, and can demonstrate, a full understanding of the local racing rules.

requirements.competency: I can clearly communicate with my crew to synchronize the helm to sail trim and to boat balance.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must be competent to right a capsized sailboat and assist others in righting other sailboats.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated proper steering techniques including smooth mark rounding, sailing by the lee and match sheeting to the turn.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must be competent to sail single-handed sailboat (e.g., Laser Radial) or a double-handed sailboat (e.g., 470, Club 420, Flying Junior, Mistral 4.04, Skiff).

requirements.competency: I can properly trim the sail of the sailboat and the crew should identify the point and speed nodes for the helm upwind.
requirements.requirement: In addition to the daysails completed for the previous five stages, Scouts must demonstrate all of the sailing skills expected of a Skipper to ensure the safety of the boat and crew, while sailing in familiar water and winds up to 15 knots.

requirements.competency: I can manoeuvre the sailboat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should maintain an International Sailing Logbook, available from Sail Canada, recording the date, location, and personal reflection of recreational, training, and competitive sailing experiences.

requirements.competency: I can recognize and treat severe hyperthermia and hypothermia.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be familiar with, and capable of, sharing their sailing knowledge with younger Scouts.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate Intermediate sailing skills.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to recognize the symptoms of weather related injuries including: • heat exhaustion and sun stroke found in hyperthermia, or • shivering, and slurring of words found in hypothermia and know how to treat them.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated the skills to steer the sailboat while underway.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated the skills to trim the sails while underway.

requirements.competency: I have successfully shown how to manoeuvre while sailing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • know what to look for choosing the favoured end of the gate and finish line while sailing • describe how lifts and headers affect their position on a course • determine the direction of current while sailing

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated down-speed opportunities.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated tactical manoeuvres while racing.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a one- to two-day local regatta.

requirements.competency: I can sail a single / double-handed boat to CANSail 4 standards.

requirements.competency: I have participated in at least seven daysail outings.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • maintain accurate body position at all points of sail, and • balance the boat by using hiking, and by moving fore and aft.

requirements.competency: I can evaluate geographical and tidal effects of a sailing venue.

requirements.competency: I can describe common seamanship knowledge.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • demonstrate how to head up and tack around the marks • demonstrate smooth tactical mark roundings

requirements.competency: I can describe common racing strategies.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • complete sail control adjustments to head up and bear away • as a crew, identify point and speed modes for helm upwind • trim the sails using all sail controls • trim the jib leech to keep top and bottom telltales breaking together • sheet the main to trim sails appropriately • depower the sails using sail controls

requirements.competency: I can describe the mechanics of air flow over the sail.

requirements.competency: I can describe how to tune a boat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • hold a position on a start line for 30 seconds • identify opportunities to sail slowly at a leeward mark

requirements.competency: I know and can describe the intent of the current ISAF racing rules 1–7.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated an understanding of sail rigging.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should participate in a local regatta in order to expand their sailing experience.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated the skills to balance the sailboat while underway the water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must be competent to sail single-handed sailboat (e.g., Laser Radial) or a double-handed sailboat (e.g., 470, Club 420, Flying Junior, Skiff).

requirements.competency: I know the Collision Regulations and proper etiquette for using a marine radio the water.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate tactics while racing.
requirements.requirement: A Scout supervising another sailboat by powerboat should have a full understanding of the Collision Regulations 1–17 and have a full understanding of the etiquette to deliver the intended messages by use of a marine radio, including, hailing for help using prefaces of either MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY, PAN-PAN PAN-PAN PAN-PAN, SECURITY SECURITY SECURITY; or on Marine Channels 9 or 16.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated racing strategies.
requirements.requirement: A Scout must be able to travel safely between sailing venues.

requirements.competency: I can sail a single-handed boat to CANSail 5 skills standards.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • describe three types of clouds and local weather patterns • and explain the major factors affecting tides and currents

requirements.competency: I have participated in at least eight daysail outings.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a regional training camp and a regional regatta.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • choose proper sail rigging for the day’s conditions • correctly rig sails each day prior to training • correctly rig the kite each day for training

requirements.competency: I have successfully completed the equivalent requirements for a CANSail 1–2 Fundamental Sailing Instructor course, from Sail Canada or its member provincial association, or internationally recognized equivalent.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to balance a boat properly while flying a spinnaker.

requirements.competency: I know how to properly prepare my boat for safe travel.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must be able to: • perform wire to wire tacks at one-minute intervals • perform 10 gybes sets at one-minute intervals • identify opportunities to bear away • perform gybe sets for a mark • perform wide and close mark rounding • steer promoting planing and surfing

requirements.competency: I can evaluate geographical and tidal effects of a new sailing venue.

requirements.competency: I know and can describe the current racing rules.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • gybe the kite by switching the pole after a gybe • gybe with the kite on command ten times at one-minute intervals • line up on a beat with a tuning partner and accelerate

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated how to tune a boat.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should able to: • perform double tacks to lay a mark and at the start line • tack to perform a close and loose cover • tack and duck under another boat • sail slowly approaching leeward marks • sail slowly approaching marks while flying a spinnaker

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated optimal body position at all times when sailing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • accelerate from the start line • identify and choose the favoured end of a gate and finish while sailing • describe three types of wind shifts

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated tacking/gybe manoeuvres.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must be competent to sail a single-handed sailboat (e.g. Laser Radial, Finn, Skiff).

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated the skills to fly a spinnaker/kite the water.
requirements.requirement: In addition to the daysails completed for the previous seven stages, Scouts must demonstrate all of the sailing skills expected of a Skipper to ensure the safety of the boat and crew, while sailing in familiar water and winds up to 18 knots.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate the skill to gybe while flying a spinnaker/kite.
requirements.requirement: I have participated in a regional training camp to hone the skills needed to SAIL to WIN and participated in a regional regatta coached by a certified CANSail 5–6 Advanced Sail Coach.

requirements.competency: I have prepared the vehicle and/or trailer with the boat that I am responsible for prior to travelling to any events.

requirements.competency: I have demonstrated at least four skills to steer the sailboat while underway the water. 9
requirements.requirement: Scouts should be able to: • inspect both the vehicle and/or the trailer and its valued racing dinghy cargo securely tied • check all vehicle brakes and signal lights to ensure they are in good working order at the time of departure to any sailing events

requirements.competency: I have successfully shown at least three manoeuvres a boat with a trapeze.
requirements.requirement: A Scout, at the advanced sailing level, traveling to compete at regional and/or provincial events, must do so safely, using precautionary driving skills according to Provincial motor vehicle regulations, and with the utmost care while carrying passengers and/or transporting sailboats.

requirements.competency: I have prepared for competition.
requirements.requirement: A Scout, at the advanced sailing level, planning for a sailing expedition or a racing event should assess the sailing event area strategically and make tactical plans to determine if there are any unique geographic features affecting local laminar wind flow, tidal activity, and flooding/ ebbing tidal currents at the time of the event.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a provincial regatta where all the skills that I have learned are consolidated into performance in order to excel during the regatta.
requirements.requirement: A Scout, racing at the advanced level, must be able to describe the implications of the I, P, Z and black flags used in racing with full understanding of their intent in accordance with the current CANSail / World Sailing Racing Rules

requirements.competency: I have successfully completed the equivalent requirements for an Intermediate CANSail 3–4 Sailing Instructor course from Sail Canada or its provincial association, or internationally recognized equivalent.
requirements.requirement: A Scout, at the advanced level, must be able to effectively evaluate and adjust both standing and running rigging prior to a race with the intent of achieving maximum performance.

requirements.competency: I can sail a double-handed boat to CANSail 5 skills standards including CANSail 5 chute skill sets.
requirements.requirement: A Scout, at the advanced sailing level, must have a full understanding of air flow over the sails, and can explain the implications of changing laminar flow on sail trim.

requirements.competency: I have used precautionary driving skills while travelling safely to events carrying passengers and/or transporting sailboats.
requirements.requirement: • tactical rounding of the mark while racing • tacking to duck under a boat • maintaining bow out on start line • consistently starting within 3 seconds of the starting horn • gybing on waves to promote planing and surfing • holding a position going upwind or downwind for a minute

requirements.competency: I have developed strategic and tactical plans prior to a race, based wind, geography, tides and currents.
requirements.requirement: • defending a position on a downwind leg • exonerating myself after a penalty by completing a 360 or a 720 during the race

requirements.competency: I can describe the implications of the I, P, Z and black flags used in racing with full understanding of their intent in accordance with the current CANSail / World Sailing Racing Rules.
requirements.requirement: • balancing the boat for the conditions, using hiking and trapezing • pushing the body out onto the trapeze before clipping in • clipping on to the trapeze using a straight arm • trapezing with feet together and shoulders back

requirements.competency: I have the knowledge to effectively evaluate and adjust both standing and running rigging prior to a race with the intent of achieving maximum performance.

requirements.competency: I have a full understanding of air flow over the sails, and can explain the implications of changing laminar flow sail trim.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate at least six steering skills.

requirements.competency: I have successfully demonstrated at least two tactical sailing manoeuvres.
