requirements.competency: I have demonstrated at least four optimal body positions while sailing a double-handed boat.
requirements.requirement: A Scout, at the advanced sailing level, should have the skills to teach Scout Sailing Stage 5, 6, 7 and 8. Scouts must have completed the equivalent standards of a CANSail 3–4 Intermediate Sailing Instructor course or internationally recognized equivalent.

skills.name: Scoutcraft Skills
skills.description: Master the traditional outdoor skills of Scouting — knots, fire, shelter, navigation, and wilderness living — from basic camp craft to advanced survival techniques.

requirements.competency: I can hang a drying line at camp with a half hitch or other knot.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can hang a line that can hold items for the whole Patrol.

requirements.competency: I can keep my mess kit clean at camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can properly clean their mess kits with hot water and soap, and properly bleach and rinse.

requirements.competency: When outdoors or at camp, I know what is drinkable (safe) and not drinkable (unsafe) water, and to check with a Scouter when I am unsure.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain where to get drinkable water at a camp and where not to get water.

requirements.competency: I know why it is important to stick to trails when outdoors.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain that they must stay on trails to keep from getting lost.

requirements.competency: I know three reasons for having a shelter when sleeping outdoors.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain that a shelter is used to keep warm, to stay out of the rain or hot sun, and to stay out of the wind.

requirements.competency: I can name three wildflowers by direct observation in a wild field, bush or forest.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can find and identify three wildflowers growing in the wild.

requirements.competency: I can gather dry, burnable wood for a fire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can find dry wood in the right sizes for a campfire (tinder, kindling, fuel).

requirements.competency: I know to tell adults where I am going when outdoors.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain why it is important to always tell an adult where they will be when in the outdoors.

requirements.competency: I know how to keep a camp clean.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must be competent to sail a double-handed sailboat (e.g. 470, Club 420, Flying Junior, Mistral 4.04, 49er) with spinnaker or kite.

requirements.competency: I can tie a reef knot, a round turn and two half-hitch knots.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can keep personal equipment clean and organized and can help keep the fire pit, kitchen, sleeping area and dining area clean and free from trash and equipment that should be stored in the proper place. Scouts can explain why a clean and tidy camp is important.

requirements.competency: I can cook a foil-wrapped meal in a fire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate each of these knots correctly and explain good uses for each.

requirements.competency: I know how much water I should carry when a hike or taking part in an outdoor activity, and I know how to carry the water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can cook a complete foil-wrapped meal, and can describe other meals that could be cooked using this method.

requirements.competency: I know what natural shelter materials or locations are to keep out of the wind, rain, sun and snow, and where these may be found.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can fill their water bottles while out hiking and know that their bottles should always be full when they start out on a hike. Scouts can explain why carrying plenty of water is important.

requirements.competency: I can identify four types of trees by direct observation in a wild field, bush or forest.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use tree bows, grasses and bushes as wind and weather breaks and covers. Scouts can also use rock formations and large trees as weather shields. Scouts can demonstrate how to incorporate natural features and materials into a constructed shelter.

requirements.competency: I have helped light a fire using only natural fire-starter materials found in the forest, and I know the safety rules for when around a campfire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can consistently identify and name four trees in the wild.

requirements.competency: I know why it is important to use a buddy system when traveling in the forest.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use a match or lighter to start the fire, but only natural material grown in the outdoors may be used as fire starter materials.

requirements.competency: I know the rules for hygiene at camp (for eating and preparing food).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain that the buddy system is helpful because if something were to happen, a Scout buddy can go for help.

requirements.competency: I can tie a half hitch, clove hitch and a fisherman’s knot.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know that they must wash and disinfect their hands before handling any food.

requirements.competency: I have put together a personal outdoor survival kit.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the three knots tied to a mastery level with a firm feel to the knot, symmetry with no twists or cross overs in the knot, with a 5–10 cm tail at the end of the knot, and can explain a good use for each.

requirements.competency: I know what makes a comfortable and safe place for a sleeping shelter or site.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use a camp stove to cook a meal for their Patrol.

requirements.competency: I can cook a meal a camp stove.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can build and maintain a pit at a campout.

requirements.competency: I can use a shovel to build a camp greywater sump pit, and close the pit when finished.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can boil a pot of water on or next to a fire. Scouts know not to poke sticks or throw trash into a fire. Scouts know not to leave a fire or hot coals unattended. Scouts know to use insulated hand protection when working with hot pots. Scouts know there is no horseplay around fires.

requirements.competency: I have boiled water over a campfire, and know the safety precautions around fires and hot pots.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know to remove the source of the heat. Scouts know that immediate cooling of the burn is required. Scouts know that infections are a risk from burns and can apply the appropriate medication and dressing to the burn.

requirements.competency: I know the first-aid treatment for burns from hot water, grease and food.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have walked for a minimum of 2 km using a compass bearing and arrived successfully at their intended destination.

requirements.competency: I have used a compass to walk a bearing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can make a waterproof shelter large enough to sleep under.

requirements.competency: I can make a personal shelter out of plastic sheeting and rope.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify three sets of animal tracks from different species in the wild.

requirements.competency: I can identify three wild animal tracks.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can lay and start a fire large enough to keep thee persons warm using only matches and natural materials found in the forest.

requirements.competency: I can lay and start a fire using no tools—only matches and natural materials found in the forest.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate a personal outdoor survival kit suitable for a particular expedition, explaining the rationale for the items included and the items left out.

requirements.competency: As part of a team, I have completed a Scoutcraft project using at least a square lashing to join two poles at right angles.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know to look for a smooth and level surface to sleep on. Scouts know to keep the site out of the wind and (where possible) precipitation. Scouts know to look up and ensure there are no hazards from falling objects such as rocks and dead trees or limbs. Scouts know to keep off of animal trails or human walking paths. Scouts know to keep the site out of low areas where water may pool or run through. Scouts know to keep a site out of a possible avalanche or landslide path.

requirements.competency: I know what to do if lost and alone outdoors with no constructed shelter available.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must have a Scouter inspect the construction before use.

requirements.competency: I can set up a tree food hang to protect my food from animals.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can tie the four knots to a mastery level with a firm feel for the knot, symmetry with no twists or crossovers in the knot, and with a 5–10 cm tail at the end of the knot where required. A rope over 8mm in diameter is to be used to demonstrate the whipping skill.

requirements.competency: I can tie a figure eight, bowline, trucker’s hitch and sheet bend, and whip the end of a rope.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can cook (by both roasting and baking over a fire) enough food to be shared with the Patrol.

requirements.competency: I can cook over a fire to roast and bake food items.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must demonstrate these skills to a Scouter to obtain a permit.

requirements.competency: I can use a knife safely (opening, closing, passing, cleaning, caring for) and have earned my knife permit.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must demonstrate this knowledge by preparing a minimum of a 1 litre bottle filled with water from a natural source.

requirements.competency: I know how to disinfect water for drinking.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can establish the cardinal points and explain how they accomplished this task.

requirements.competency: I can establish the four cardinal directions (north, south, east, west) without a magnetic compass or any electronic means.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can construct a shelter that protects from wind, rain, sun and snow.

requirements.competency: I can build a personal sleeping shelter out of snow or any other natural materials.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can safely identify three edible wild plants in a natural setting and context. Scouters must approve all plants before they are consumed by Scouts.

requirements.competency: I can safely identify three edible wild plants. (Note: Scouters must approve all plants before they are consumed by Scouts.)
requirements.requirement: Within five minutes (timed), Scouts can build a fire that should be burning sufficiently so that it does not require fuel or manipulation to remain reliably burning.

requirements.competency: I can quickly (under 5 minutes) build an emergency warming fire for a group of three persons without using tools (only matches).
requirements.requirement: Scouts know: • To not panic and try to stay calm. • To call out for help or use a signaling device such as whistle or phone. • To retrace their steps, if possible—otherwise, stay put in place. • To try to find or make a shelter out of the wind and precipitation. • To dress warmly; light a warming and signal fire if possible. • To spread out visible material on the ground for air searchers to see.

requirements.competency: I have built a lean-to shelter and an A-frame sleeping tripod shelter, using wood and tied with four lashing knots: square, diagonal, tripod and shear lashings.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can set up a hang that can support at least 5 kilograms of weight.

requirements.competency: I have dehydrated 1000 calories of food and taken it a camping trip as my trail snack for two days.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can build both of these shelters, suitable for one person to sleep in.

requirements.competency: I have cooked with cast-iron cookware (or substitute cookware materials) by placing cookware in the fire or coals as well as top of the fire or coals.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can cook a meal over a fire or coals using cast iron cookware suitable to feed a minimum of three persons.

requirements.competency: I know how to use a camp axe and camp folding saw or bow saw safely (opening, closing, passing, cleaning, caring for, sharpening, cutting), and have obtained the appropriate permits for these.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate axe and saw skills consistently over a campout.

requirements.competency: I have maintained and used a commercial backpacking portable water-treatment device, and know the limitations, advantages and disadvantages of these devices.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate these skills over a campout or a two-day trail outing.

requirements.competency: I have navigated, all off trail, 3 km to a predetermined fixed point in a wilderness area using a magnetic compass bearing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to demonstrate this skill by leading a Patrol on an off-trail outing over 3 km using a magnetic compass bearing.

requirements.competency: I have built and slept out at least two nights in a shelter big enough for three persons and made of only natural materials found outdoors and rope.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can construct a shelter suitable enough to keep out wind and precipitation, and the shelter has been used over at least two nights.

requirements.competency: I have caught, cleaned and cooked a fish over a campfire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts may use any tools or equipment they can carry in a backpack to accomplish this task. Scouts must follow local regulations with regard to both fishing and campfires.

requirements.competency: I have laid and lit a teepee fire, pyramid fire, star fire and reflector fire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to demonstrate all four of these fires to a level that is suitable enough to warm one person.

requirements.competency: I have built an improvised stretcher out of rope, overnight backpacking camping equipment and natural materials found outdoors.

requirements.competency: I have built a usable Burma/Monkey bridge.

requirements.competency: From wood I have not harvested from a live source, I have carved and used my own hiking “Scout” staff a trail hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can construct a bridge a minimum of 5 metres long. The bridge can be tested, but should not be used high off the ground.

requirements.competency: I have built and cooked a personal-sized stove only made out of tin cans, wax, candle wicks and cardboard (a buddy stove).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate their stove’s capacity to boil a pot of water for a hot drink.

requirements.competency: Using a knife and axe, I have prepared a 10-person campfire with tinder, kindling and fuel logs gathered from a forest floor. The fire burned for 5 hours with all wood gathered before the fire was lit (no gathering additional fire wood once the fire is lit and burning).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate this skill at an evening campout fire.

requirements.competency: I have built a solar still and collected at least one cup of drinking water from the still.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate this skill to their Patrol.

requirements.competency: I have navigated to and found 10 geocache locations.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use any type of navigational aids, but they need to explain how they navigated to the cache sites.

requirements.competency: I have constructed and slept two nights in an igloo, quinzhee or trench snow shelter capable of sleeping three persons.
requirements.requirement: Scouts demonstrating this skill do not need to use their shelter on consecutive nights, and they do not need to share their shelter with two other Scouts overnight.

requirements.competency: I have identified 15 bird species in the wild using a written birding record journal.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify 15 bird species in the wild using a written birding record journal that includes information such as species name, habitat, weather, date and time, appearance, behaviour, flock size, etc.

requirements.competency: I can light a fire using only mechanical means (flint and steel, ferrocerium striker or friction e.g. bow and drill).
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to be able to light a three-person warming fire.

requirements.competency: I know how to send a signal for help, without any electronic means, in four different ways that can be observed by air searchers.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the four air search signal methods.

requirements.competency: Using spars (poles) and rope, I have constructed a three-metre-high or a bridge over a three-metre span.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use the staff on at least five hiking days.

requirements.competency: I can make a marine rescue Mayday radio call using the required procedures and voice script.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can complete this construction with the help of other Patrol members.

requirements.competency: I have constructed and used an improvised or commercial solar shower at camp or a camping trip.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can teach the knots to a mastery level with a firm feel for the knot, symmetry with no twists or crossovers in the knot, with a 5–10 cm tail at the end of the knot as required.

requirements.competency: I have taught five knots to younger Scout members.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the stove and how it works.

requirements.competency: I have made a vagabond (tin can) stove and cooked a personal camp meal it.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can build all these systems and demonstrate the function by moving a 20 kilogram load.

requirements.competency: I can construct a 2:1, 3:1, and 4:1 rope pulley system to raise or move loads or tension lines.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the use of this newspaper material to keep three persons warm.

requirements.competency: I have made newspapers fire logs and bricks, sufficient to have a three-hour warming fire indoors in a stove or fireplace or outside with a campfire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the poisonous flora and fauna in their part of Canada.

requirements.competency: I can identify the poisonous living organisms, animals and plants in Canada.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can produce the water over several days of a campout.

requirements.competency: I have made and used a solar snow-melt reflector or absorber to melt enough snow to generate 2 litres of drinking water.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can teach these lessons over multiple days.

requirements.competency: Using a topographic map and magnetic compass, I have taught younger Scouts to determine their location a map and to make their way to another location a map.
requirements.requirement: Scouts do not have to complete the five days of work at one time.

requirements.competency: I have organized a Scout group and participated in five days of trail or tent campsite construction, maintenance or clean-up work.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate this knowledge and refer to and use the Transport Canada Radio Distress Call script.

requirements.competency: I can backsplice, short splice and eye splice a three-strand rope.
requirements.requirement: The shower can be used over several days.

requirements.competency: I have made 4 metres of 3-strand cordage from only natural plant products found outdoors.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must show the splices to their Patrol mates.

requirements.competency: I can cook a complete campout meal using only improvised natural cooking surfaces and devices (cooking only using flat stones, wood planks, cooking hooks, spits, covered pits or trenches).
requirements.requirement: Scouts must cook a meal for a minimum of three persons.

requirements.competency: I have carved cooking and eating spoons and a bowl with carving tools (or a knife) and wood.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to use these carved items at a campout.

requirements.competency: I have given a lesson to Scout youth obtaining drinkable water in the wilderness all year round.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to provide the lesson in one session to young Scouts, covering concepts including: water filtration, disinfecting methods, locating, daily and activity hydration amount requirements, storing and carrying in winter and non-winter months, hazards of untreated water.

requirements.competency: I can find the North Star and identify four of the constellations in the northern sky.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to provide this identification to their Patrol mates during an evening outing.

requirements.competency: With only materials found in the forest, rope and plastic sheeting, I have built a 10-person campout dinning shelter, protected all sides from the weather.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to use this shelter at a campout.

requirements.competency: I have observed (and photographed as proof) a total of 15 North American mammals, reptiles and amphibians in the wild.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to share these photos with their Patrol.

requirements.competency: Using only natural materials, I can light and maintain a fire in falling rain or falling snow conditions.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to demonstrate these fire skills to their Patrol.

requirements.competency: I know how to set up and lead a ground search exercise for a missing person using a hasty search and a grid search, all in an area of a minimum of 2 km2 in size.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to set up and run a search proactive scenario with their Patrol.

requirements.competency: I have built a large temporary pole and lashing “gateway” entrance (with flag pole) for a jamboree or campout activity site.
requirements.requirement: Scouts do not have to produce the cordage as one complete strand.

requirements.competency: Using only wood as a fuel source, I have built a reflector camp oven and cooked a meal for a minimum of five persons, including both roasted and baked food preparation.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can construct this gateway with the assistance of their Patrol.

requirements.competency: I have built and used an improvised potable water filter.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can provide this meal over several meal times.

requirements.competency: Using GPS devices, I have set up and facilitated a ten-station outdoor orienteering course activity for Scouts.
requirements.requirement: Any type of filter can be made as long as 10 litres of water can be filtered.

requirements.competency: I have planted a minimum of 100 new trees.

requirements.competency: I have built and used a Leave No Trace warming fire for 10 persons for three hours.
requirements.requirement: Scouts do not have to complete this planting at one time.

requirements.competency: I have led a team an evacuation simulation exercise of a victim being carried out at the minimum 3 km of trail using only an improvised stretcher (Note: It is expected youth to have training in wilderness first aid before undertaking this activity).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can ensure no markings are left on the ground (once it is out and disassembled).

requirements.competency: I have run a field camp kitchen for two days or more, for a Cub Scout (or younger) group.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can conduct this exercise with the utmost in safety in mind. There should be more than adequate persons to address fatigue issues with stretcher bearers. There is to be an improvised mock casualty in the stretcher that weighs the equivalent of an adult victim.

skills.name: Trail Skills
skills.description: Build confidence navigating the outdoors on foot, from day hikes to multi-night backcountry expeditions using maps, compasses, and GPS.

requirements.competency: I can help pack a rucksack for a day hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can indicate the items to bring for a day hike. Scouts can explain how to pack soft items, heavy items and food items. Scouts can explain what is meant by “first in-last out” when packing items. Scouts can demonstrate the wet weather equipment to bring on a day hike.

requirements.competency: I have attended at least two hikes.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how weather affects what can be worn for a day hike. Scouts can explain the concept of layering and understand what to wear for wet weather. Scouts can demonstrate the types of footwear needed for a day hike in each season.

requirements.competency: I can dress myself for a day hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the food groups to bring for a day hike to sustain and boost energy. Scouts can show what to bring for snacks. Scouts can show how to keep drinks hot or cold. Scouts can explain what dehydration means and can show how to bring water on a day hike.

requirements.competency: I can list what food to bring a day hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain why they must listen to the Scouter so that everyone is kept safe on a day hike.

requirements.competency: I can follow directions a day hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify the parts of a compass outdoors in a practical experience.

requirements.competency: I can identify the main parts of a compass.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe safety rules and procedures that will keep themselves and their group safe while on a day hike. Scouts can explain how their own behaviour affects others on the day hike. Scouts can demonstrate how to hike on various types of terrain (smooth, rough), how to keep up with others and the importance of always being able to see a Scouter while on the day hike.

requirements.competency: I can behave safely while hiking.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how they contribute to the success of the day hike by being members of the team. Scouts can describe how their awareness will help everyone have a safe day hike.

requirements.competency: I can be responsible for myself while we are hiking.
requirements.requirement: Scouts explain how and why the buddy system is used on a day hike.

requirements.competency: I can explain the buddy system.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can draw and identify the main distress signals.

requirements.competency: I can recognize the main distress signals.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can operate the camp kitchen with assistance from other Patrol members.

requirements.competency: I know what gear to bring depending the weather.
requirements.requirement: As much as possible these can be different types of day hikes, such as in neighborhoods, parks, open natural areas, etc.

requirements.competency: I can show how to take care of all personal gear needed for a day hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how weather varies from one location to another (from flat urban areas to wooded areas, valleys to hilltops) and how that will affect what gear to bring on a day hike. Scouts can explain the gear needed for downpour, high winds, sun and humidity. Scouts can explain the importance of bringing personal protection materials needed for insects and sun.

requirements.competency: I can read a simple map.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the value of gear needed for hikes and the importance of caring for it. Scouts can check their equipment prior to the hike. Scouts show how to put away and store equipment after the hike.

requirements.competency: I can use a compass to find basic directions.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can read a simply drawn map of the neighbourhood (e.g. meeting area, local park etc.). Scouts can demonstrate how to orient a map and how to follow a simple route around the map. Scouts can explain how different colours on a map define the areas (water, urban, park, forest) and can identify symbols used for roads, trails, buildings, rivers, etc.

requirements.competency: I can obtain a weather forecast.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate basic use of the compass: how to take a bearing and how to follow a bearing. Scouts can demonstrate how to use a map and compass together to navigate an area.

requirements.competency: I can be a responsible member of my team while we are hiking.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can list methods to obtain a weather forecast. Scouts can explain why Scouts need to watch forecasts ahead of a hike.

requirements.competency: I can get help if someone is hurt.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify some risk concerns that might be present on a day hike and offer safety procedures to counteract them. Scouts can explain how to be a member of the team while on a hike and how their behaviour will impact the hike and experience of others.

requirements.competency: I have attended at least three hikes.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can recognize when someone is injured and needs help. Scouts can explain how to gethelp in various conditions.

requirements.competency: I can explain why one brings certain foods and drinks hikes.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have attended at least three hikes in natural areas such as parks, forests and Scout camps.

requirements.competency: I can pack a rucksack for a day hike.

requirements.competency: I can treat simple cuts and scratches.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can list the equipment, including team equipment to bring with them for a day hike. Scouts can show how to pack equipment in their rucksack: heavy items, soft items, last in-first out principle, food items and water.

requirements.competency: I know how to avoid becoming lost, and I know what to do if I get lost.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how the weather can change very quickly and how they need to plan in advance. Scouts can explain the layering system, outer shell; how to control body head and ventilation; wicking principle.

requirements.competency: I have attended at least three hiking activities, one of which involves hiking hilly trails.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can follow a simple orienteering trail using an orienteering map.

requirements.competency: I can explain what clothes to bring for a day hike depending the weather.

requirements.competency: I can follow a route an orienteering map.

requirements.competency: I can identify the features of a topographical map.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the use of a compass to another youth including: taking a bearing, following a bearing and orienting a map with a compass.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate the basic use of a GPS unit.

requirements.competency: I can teach another youth how to find direction by using a compass.

requirements.competency: I can explain the effect of weather hiking activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the Leave No Trace principles in action when on a hike, including: waste reduction, respect for animals, minimizing impact on the trail, consideration for others. Scouts can explain how urination and defecation are handled on the trail.

requirements.competency: I can be responsible for myself and aware of my surroundings while hiking.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to clean the wound, apply a bandage and reassure the patient.

requirements.competency: I can explain the main principles of Leave No Trace.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how to prevent becoming lost and what to do if they are lost alone or as a group.

requirements.competency: I can teach another youth what to pack for a day hike.

requirements.competency: I can cross various terrains such as rocky or wet ground.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can show other Scouts how to pack the equipment in their rucksack: heavy items, soft items, last in-first out principle, food items and water.

requirements.competency: I can apply the Leave No Trace principles while hiking.

Scouts can show how to pack heavy items, soft items, food, fuel, stoves, and their share of team equipment. Scouts can explain the types of eye protection needed for various conditions, such as: sunglasses, glacier glasses and snow goggles. Scouts can list and describe the 10 essential items to always have in their rucksacks. According to AdventureSmart, they are: • Flashlight • Navigational/communication aids • Fire-making kit • First aid kit • Whistle or mirror • Extra food and water • Emergency shelter • Pocket knife • Extra clothing • Sun protection

requirements.competency: I can minimize trail hazards for myself and others that are encountered (trip hazards the trail, minimizing branch whip while moving them out of the way, etc.—trail etiquette).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the value of maintaining equipment and demonstrate how to check and care for their equipment including: safety considerations, keeping equipment in working condition, checking in advance, simple repairs and cleaning.

requirements.competency: I can be responsible for younger members of my team while we are hiking.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe how to keep food safe for all members of the group and avoid cross-contamination. Scouts can explain how to recognize and treat allergies, including anaphylactic reactions.

requirements.competency: I can treat simple sprains and blisters.

Scouts can demonstrate the use of a compass to determine bearings for the route. Scouts can plot locations based on a grid references, calculate distances and changes in height.

requirements.competency: I can identify the different emergency services that are available and how and when to call them.
requirements.requirement: Scouts demonstrate to others how to follow a pre-defined route on a map.

requirements.competency: I can build or find an emergency shelter.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe and use the various methods of keeping a map dry and safe: zip-lock bags, laminating, map cases and map coatings.

requirements.competency: I have attended three hikes (including an overnight).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to locate a pre-programmed waypoint.

requirements.competency: I can lead a leg of a hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate methods of obtaining the forecast for the hiking area. Scouts can show how to prepare for the various weather conditions that may be encountered on the hike.

requirements.competency: I can help plan a day hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how to cross various terrains safely. Scouts can cross wet bogs or marshes safely and minimize their impact on the environment.

requirements.competency: I can pack a rucksack for a weekend hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate their knowledge of the Leave No Trace principles by disposing of waste properly, respecting wildlife, minimizing the impacts of hiking and fire, showing consideration of others and hiking and camping on durable surfaces whenever possible.

requirements.competency: I can show how to care for all my personal hiking equipment needed for a weekend hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate hiking etiquette on the trail. Scouts know what to do if encountering other hikers on a trail. Scouts can avoid livestock or wildlife on a trail, and know what to do if animals are encountered.

requirements.competency: I know how to plan for and avoid food allergies in a group hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have a level of awareness to help younger or less- experienced Scouts while hiking.

requirements.competency: I can use a map and compass together for navigation.

Scouts can make the patient feel safe and know how to get help. Scouts know the materials in a first aid kit that are used to treat a blister and demonstrate their skill.

requirements.competency: I can teach another youth how to follow a route an orienteering map.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how to call for emergency services in the area in which Scouts are hiking (police, ambulance, search etc.). Scouts are able to explain what each service would provide in a hiking situation.

requirements.competency: I can keep a map dry and safe from the elements.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to erect a simple emergency shelter or explain natural formations that could be used for emergency shelters.

requirements.competency: I can locate a waypoint that has been pre- programmed into a GPS unit.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have attended at least three hikes in wilderness-type areas different from those experienced in other stages. One of the hikes is to be an overnight experience.

requirements.competency: I can plan and bring appropriate personal gear to use a hike based expected weather forecasts for the hiking area.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can take the lead position on a section of a hike. Scouts will demonstrate how to navigate, support, guide and lead others over the trail.

requirements.competency: I can explain how the weather affects the equipment I bring a weekend hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have been involved in the selection of season and location for a day hike.

requirements.competency: I can teach another youth the basic use of a GPS unit.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how the weather can change very quickly and how they need to plan in advance. Scouts can explain the layering system, outer shell; how to control body head and ventilation; wicking principle. Scouts can demonstrate how to keep their pack and pack contents dry in wet and snow conditions.

requirements.competency: I can find directions without a compass.

requirements.competency: I can describe the dangers of weather hikes.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate proper use of equipment while supervised on a hike.

requirements.competency: I can plan effectively and recommend appropriate gear to take for my group based weather forecasts for the hiking area.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe appropriate footwear and comfort factors such as ankle support, sole support and construction materials. Scouts can explain the use of gaiters when hiking.

requirements.competency: I know when and how to cross a river.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the different types of stoves and fuel that can be used on day and overnight hikes.

requirements.competency: I can show how and explain when to use the main distress signals.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe how to keep food, food containers and food utensils hygienic and how to handle food safely during the hike and while preparing meals.

requirements.competency: I can be an active member of my team while hiking.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate their skills with a map and compass while supervised on the trail. Scouts can show how to find their position on the map with reference to their surroundings and local features. Scouts can take bearings of surrounding areas and find their position.

requirements.competency: I can recognize and respond to hazards from flora and fauna.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to plot a hike route and use a compass to obtain the necessary bearings. Scouts can orient the map using features on a map and use the features to plot a hiking route.

requirements.competency: I have taken part in three hikes (including an overnight).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to use a GPS by entering waypoints and navigating to the destination.

requirements.competency: I have written a log for at least two of these activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to use a GPS to other Scouts.

requirements.competency: I can show what group equipment to bring a weekend hike and explain why each item is needed.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate various methods of finding direction: using the sun, stars, shadows, a watch, the moon.

requirements.competency: I can help plan an overnight hike.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain how temperature changes and changes in wind speed and direction can indicate weather changes. Scouts can describe how cloud cover, mist, fog and snow can affect the hike and how to be proactively safe.

requirements.competency: I can help choose a suitable hiking destination.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can list the gear needed for the hike and the adjustments required depending on weather forecasts. Scouts can distinguish between regular group gear and safety/ emergency use gear.

requirements.competency: I can hike steep trails safely, using appropriate gear as required.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to cross a river using various methods, including the preferred safe crossing on a bridge or other designated safe crossing areas. Scouts can explain safety procedures including how to protect clothes so youth stay dry, and steps to keep warm.

requirements.competency: I can show how to use group equipment correctly.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate distress signals while supervised outdoors in both daytime and night-time conditions.

requirements.competency: I can select appropriate footwear for various hikes.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate awareness of trail conditions and ways to support other members of the team. Scouts can explain the various roles required for a safe hiking experience. Scouts can describe proper trail pacing for a group and how to schedule rest and water breaks. Scouts can explain the role of the front leader and rear follower on a hike.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate how to use different types of lightweight stoves to prepare a meal.
requirements.requirement: Scouts demonstrate their knowledge of flora and fauna that could be hazardous on the trail. Scouts can explain how to recognize these hazards, how to avoid the hazards and how to respond to exposure.

requirements.competency: I can keep food and food preparation materials hygienic.

requirements.competency: I can demonstrate the limitations of the compass and other navigation tools.

requirements.competency: I can teach another youth how to find his or her position the ground using a map and compass.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can list the items that are needed for safety and emergency use for a weekend hike, including safety ropes, sleeping materials, dry bags, shelters and first aid kits.

requirements.competency: I can recognize changing weather patterns while hiking.
requirements.requirement: Scouts demonstrate to other youth how to look after group equipment.

requirements.competency: I can show different methods for crossing waterways.
requirements.requirement: Scouts demonstrate proper cleaning and storage of their footwear, including waterproofing.

requirements.competency: I can teach the principles of Leave No Trace.
requirements.requirement: Scouts explain how to choose a pack, fit it, name the key parts, and know how to make repairs while on the trail.

requirements.competency: I can recognize unstable or elevated risk areas (e.g. slick trails, icy terrain) and either avoid them or minimize the risk in crossing the area with accepted use of gear and technique.

requirements.competency: I can recognize and treat hypothermia, hyperthermia, sunstroke, dehydration and asthma, or other medical conditions relevant to my team.
requirements.requirement: Scouts demonstrate how to use water purifiers and identify suitable natural sources from which to obtain water.

requirements.competency: I can be responsible for myself and my team while hiking.

requirements.competency: I can describe the limitations of my team.

requirements.competency: I have written logs for all of these activities.

skills.name: Vertical Skills
skills.description: Discover the vertical world through climbing and rappelling, progressing from your first wall to leading multi-pitch climbs on natural rock and ice.

requirements.competency: I know the safety rules for climbing rocks, trees, fences and man-made structures.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know to always climb with an adult’s permission and an adult present. Scouts do not climb on fences. Scouts climb in playgrounds on play equipment—not on inappropriate structures. Scouts get permission from an adult before climbing trees.

requirements.competency: I have been to a jungle-gym playground and know how to play safely and cooperatively with others.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use their knowledge from other expeditions to lead the financial planning and budgeting portion of a hiking expedition.

requirements.competency: I have been climbing an artificial wall or natural rock formation.
requirements.requirement: Scouts always take turns and share the equipment. Scouts do not climb on other people. Scouts tell adults when they are going to play at the playground. Scouts do not jump down from high places.

requirements.competency: I know the safety rules for being at the top of or the bottom of a cliff face.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have tried either climbing in the context of a supervised “bouldering” or a top rope climbing lesson.

requirements.competency: I can properly put a climbing helmet.

Scouts do not play, rest or stand below a cliff face where rocks, dirt and other things could fall down. Scouts always have a climbing helmet on when at the bottom of a climbing site. Scouts never push or throw anything over a cliff edge.

requirements.competency: I can identify and name the parts of a carabiner.

requirements.competency: I have correctly tied a figure-8 follow-through knot.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify the gate, spine, nose, hinge, latch, basket (where the rope sits), crotch (where the attachment connects), barrel and spring on a carabiner.

requirements.competency: I know when and where I should have a climbing helmet on.

requirements.competency: I know the safety rules for climbing or rappelling an artificial climbing wall.

requirements.competency: I know how to care for, handle and store a climbing rope, harness, helmet and climbing webbing.

requirements.competency: I know the basic Leave No Trace rules for an outdoor climbing site.

requirements.competency: I have completed a climb to the top of an artificial climbing wall.

requirements.competency: I can put and adjust a climbing harness.

requirements.competency: I know the safety rules for participating an aerial or ropes challenge course.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can put a harness on with the leg loops and waist belt adjusted properly and securely tightened.

requirements.competency: I know the difference between: a. single-pitch, b. multi-pitch, c. top-rope, d. lead climbing e. seconding climbing methods.

requirements.requirement: Scouts know what to inspect on each of these items for wear, damage, improper working condition and missing components. Helmet: all rivets and fasteners secure, all webbing in good condition, the shell is without cracks or defects, all buckles present and working, all size adjustment functions working. Harness: all webbing in good condition with no cuts, defects or abrasions, all buckles present and in good condition with no cracks, defects or burs, all buckle adjustments working. Rope: rope has no constrictions or blowouts; no mantle fibers showing through the kern; no abraded, cut or melted kern fibers; dynamic properties are intact; rope is the proper length (usually 60–70 metres); rope is not overly soiled by dirt or foreign contaminates such as oils or chemicals. Carabiners: all working parts present, gate opens freely without sticking, on a locking gate the barrel works freely, no cracks of defects in the material, no metal burs or sharp edges.

requirements.competency: I understand the concepts, principles, physics and consequences of shock loading in climbing.
requirements.requirement: The Scout belayer can provide a continuous belay to a climber from the start to the finish of the climb when the climber unties from the rope. The knowledge and skill of attaching and detaching the belay device to the rope is not required.

requirements.competency: I can coil a climbing rope using a butterfly and a mountaineer method.

Tension by the belay safety rope is not required during the rappel, but a minimum of slack should be in the belay rope.

The harness is to be tied to the body with the correct leg loop and waist tightness. A complete top rope climb and lower back down is to be made with the harness on. requirements.competency: I have attached a friction (non-moving part) rappel device to a rope and harness and used the device to rappel.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the rules for bouldering: wear a helmet, do not boulder alone or unsupervised, do not climb with feet over waist height, use a partner to spot climbers and have a crash pad in place.

requirements.competency: I can lower a climber a top rope down to the ground.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use rocks, trees and man-made objects to as anchor points. Natural and man-made attachments must be inspected by Scouts for security. Scouts must use properly mastered and tied knots (bowline, water knot, overhand, full strength tie off, etc.). Scouts can demonstrate the use of manufactured passive climbing protection: nuts, hexes, stoppers, cams, etc. Scouts can explain and correctly apply the principles of the anchor building acronym “SARENE-SA” (Solid Anchors, Redundant, Equalized, No Extension, Small Angles).

requirements.competency: I have constructed and climbed in a “Swiss seat” improvised climbing harness made with tubular or tape webbing.
requirements.requirement: in their climbing practice demonstrate the climbing Leave No principles. Ahead Pick a climb that suits the skill level of your group to minimize the possibility of injury and need of rescue. Use appropriate equipment thoroughly checked before the climb. Find out about permits and practices; some locations do not allow drilling or anchors, or require permits. Carpool to minimize overcrowding at the trailhead. Surfaces Ensure the staging area is large enough to accommodate your group; do not forget other groups or users may be also present. Use quick draws to reduce wear on existing permanent anchors. When bouldering, ensure the ground is durable so spotters or crash pads will not destroy vegetation. Removal of rocks or landscaping to make a bouldering problem safe should be avoided. Popular climbing routes have established descent trails—use them. Do not wrap rope around trees where the friction can destroy the bark. Instead, tie a sling around the tree and run your rope through the sling with a rappel ring or carabiner. of Waste Properly Pack out worn out or discarded gear (such as old webbing or discarded tape). Minimize chalk dust; keep chalk bags closed to prevent spills. Clean chalk spills. Human waste is a problem around popular climbing areas. Go to the bathroom on the way to the climb to avoid the problem altogether.

requirements.competency: I know the safety rules for “bouldering” climbing.
requirements.requirement: Urinate well away from the climbing site location; high concentrations of urine at the site make for an unpleasant environment for all. Soil is often thin with little vegetative growth in rocky, arid or alpine climbing areas. Pack out all human waste. Create a human waste pack-out kit or use a commercial one; burying waste results in high concentrations of human waste catholes in a small, highly used area. What You Find Use removable climbing protection as much as possible. Use fixed protection sparingly; for fixed protection, use earth-coloured webbing and coloured bolt hangers. Before placing bolts, check with local land managers; it may not be legal or it may be required to use a hand drill rather than motorized drill. If climbing a new route, avoid lichen-covered rock, vegetated cracks and areas that require cleaning. Leave the rocks in place rather than force a route that will leave a noticeable path. Wildlife Critical nesting sites are found in cliff faces for many birds; other animals use rock outcrops for shelter. Be aware of seasonal rock site closures (mandatory and voluntary). Keep alert for animals protecting their homes; change or abandon climbing routes to leave animals their space. Be careful placing hands and feet; do not accidentally destroy a nest or get bitten by hidden wildlife and insects. Considerate of Other Visitors Consider climbing on weekdays or less-popular times. Wear earth-tone clothes to minimize your visual impact while scurrying up a cliff face. Minimize noise while waiting to climb or hanging out with your group. Give other climbing parties plenty of room and time to climb at their pace, or politely ask if you can pass when it is convenient and safe.
