requirements.competency: I have belayed a rappeller from above a rappel site.
requirements.requirement: Scouts demonstrate they can visually and physically check bolt anchors by: • looking for loose rock material around the bolt placement—bolt attached to rock that looks loose or broken, sounds “hollow” when hit with a rock hammer, 30 cm of solid rock on all sides of a bolt is ideal • checking the bolt and hanger—hanger held tight, no large amount of rust on metal parts, no cracks or bends in metal, nuts are secure • Bolt and hanger are of modern-era type. Scouts can describe what modern bolt hangers and bolts look like, and what older designs look like.

requirements.competency: I have set up a climbing-anchor systems using active protection.
requirements.requirement: The climbing Scout is to be belayed on a top rope belay safety rope. The climbing Scout is also tied into a second rope belayed from the ground up. This rope is to be clipped into carabiners attached to pre-set protection placements on the climbing route as the climber ascends. The climbing Scout is to be belayed with the ground up belay in a fashion consistent with a method used as if the Scout was making an actual non-mock lead climb.

requirements.competency: I know how to assess and use in-place climbing bolt anchors, bolt hangers and fixed permanent rappel and belay stations.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can set up one of each of a tube, auto-locking and auto-blocking belay device, attached to a climbing rope and used during a climb by the Scout belayer.

requirements.competency: I have completed a mock lead climb as the climber an artificial climbing wall.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the deference between flat and tubular webbing and the different widths and strengths of webbing. Scouts can explain the differences between nylon, Dyneema and Spectra webbing materials and the advantages and disadvantages of these . webbing fabrics Scouts can make a self-constructed climbing sling with a water knot. Scouts can describe what a commercially sewn climbing sling is.

requirements.competency: I have set up and used a tube, auto-locking and auto-blocking belay device.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe what a fall factor is in climbing and the hazards associated. Scouts can explain how to minimize fall factors.

requirements.competency: I have completed a climbing route over a 30 degree incline, either an ice climb, glacier or frozen snow field.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe a “UIAA” certified climbing rope. Scouts can describe a standard length of a climbing rope (60–70 metres). Scouts can describe the size and use of a single climbing rope: 9 to 11 mm diameter for climbing with a single rope, because larger diameter single ropes can take a greater fall and are more resistant to wear and abrasion, but large ropes are heavier. Smaller diameter ropes are lighter and better for multi-pitch longer climbing. 8–9 mm diameter ropes offer a full rope length for rappelling, produce less impact force in a fall and offer less chance of both ropes being cut in a rock fall.

requirements.competency: I have constructed and used a highline (also called Tyrolean Travers or Aerial Runway).
requirements.requirement: The completed climb can be a top rope, second or lead climb and is to be a climb of a minimum of a half a rope length.

requirements.competency: I have been caving or a via ferrata.
requirements.requirement: The construction it to be out of engineered purpose-built climbing equipment. The weight of one person must be moved from one side of the highline to another.

requirements.competency: I have completed a single pitch mock lead climb a natural (not constructed) climbing site.
requirements.requirement: Notes: Via ferrata routes are offered by commercial companies and can be found in both the east and west of Canada. Nationally, caving is generally controlled by a small number of caving clubs. It is best for Scouts to contact one of these clubs to obtain assistance for this requirement. There are caving clubs in both the east and west of Canada.

requirements.competency: I have used utility cord and a friction knot (or system) as protection a rappel.
requirements.requirement: The climbing Scout is belayed on a top rope belay safety rope. The climbing Scout is also tied into a second rope belayed from the ground up; this rope is to be clipped into carabiners attached to pre- set protection placements on the climbing route. The climbing Scout is to be belayed with a ground up belay in a fashion consistent with a method used as if the Scout was making an actual non-mock lead climb.

requirements.competency: I know the difference between a dynamic and static climbing rope, and where and when these rope types are used.
requirements.requirement: Scouts demonstrate the use of a Prusik knot or other suitable friction knot that attaches the climber to the rappel rope as a redundant back up in case of rappel device failure/malfunction or loss of control by the rappeller.

requirements.competency: I have climbed at a natural (not constructed) top rope climbing site where I set up all the anchor and belay systems for the routes I have climbed or rappelled on.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the climbing activities for which dynamic and static climbing ropes are suitable. Scouts can describe the functional differences of dynamic and static climbing rope.

requirements.competency: I have seconded a multi-pitch rock climb of grade 5.7 or higher.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can set up anchor and belay systems to the satisfaction of a qualified climbing instructor before use.

requirements.competency: I have seconded an ice climb of W3 or higher.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can follow a lead climber on a route and clean all the protection from the route.

requirements.competency: I have assisted with setting up a top rope climbing site and assisted with managing beginner climbers learning to climb at that site.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can follow a lead climber on a route and clean all the protection from the route.

requirements.competency: I have assisted with setting up a rappel site with a belay from above, and assisted with managing beginners learning to rappel at that site.
requirements.requirement: Scouts (under the supervision of a qualified climbing instructor) can help to facilitate the technical set up and teaching of beginner top rope climbers.

requirements.competency: I have taught younger Scouts to tie these seven knots: follow-through figure-eight, double fisherman’s, water knot, bowline, Munter hitch, Prusik and clove hitch.
requirements.requirement: Scouts (under the supervision of a qualified climbing instructor) can help to facilitate the technical set up and teaching of a beginner-level rappel site.

requirements.competency: I have set up a top rope climbing site and managed or instructed beginner climbers learning to climb at that site.
requirements.requirement: Scouts are to teach these seven knots to a beginner level only. The knots are not to be used for climbing or rappelling activities.

requirements.competency: I have set up a rappel site with a belay from above the rappeller and managed or instructed beginners learning to rappel at that site.
requirements.requirement: Scouts must have achieved the status of a qualified top rope climbing instructor to offer this service. Scouts must work under the direct supervision of a qualified climbing instructor with five or more years of climbing instruction experience.

requirements.competency: I have completed a lead climb one of the following multi-pitch climbs: 1. rock climb grade 5.7 or above 2. ice climb grade WI3 or above 3. mountain alpine climb grade III or above 4. caving that requires climbing ropes and harness
requirements.requirement: Scouts must have achieved the status of a qualified top rope climbing instructor to offer this service. Scouts must work under the direct supervision of a qualified climbing instructor with five or more years of climbing instruction experience.

requirements.competency: I have set up and used a Munter mule combination hitch to facilitate the rescue of beginners learning to rappel.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can complete a lead climb under the direct supervision of a qualified climbing instructor with five or more years of experience.

skills.name: Winter Skills
skills.description: Embrace the Canadian winter — learn to dress, travel, camp, and thrive in cold and snowy conditions, from your first winter hike to leading multi-day expeditions.

requirements.competency: I have built something out of snow.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will demonstrate an ability to have fun in the outdoors (even in the cold of winter) by building something out of snow such as a snowman, a snow fort, or some kind of snow sculpture. This can be completed as a group activity or individually. It is preferable that this be completed as part of a Scouting activity with the Scout’s team.

requirements.competency: I have hiked at least 1 km in winter.
requirements.requirement: The Scout will know how to pack a nutritious and tasty snack appropriate for the outing to eat outdoors, bearing in mind that activities in winter require more energy than in other seasons. Snacks should consider the environment and should minimize garbage.

requirements.competency: I know that I should not touch cold metal with bare skin, especially my lips or tongue.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should routinely drink water when on outdoor activities and should recognize that being grumpy and tired are early signs of dehydration.

requirements.competency: I have prepared a good snack for a winter outing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can keep water bottles from freezing by keeping them under their coats or using an insulated water bottle or water bottle insulating sleeve.

requirements.competency: I always carry a water bottle when going a winter outing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will have participated in an outdoor sports or games day, ideally with their Patrol or Section.

requirements.competency: I can keep my water bottle from freezing a winter outing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts should demonstrate a basic understanding of how to dress when going outside in the wintertime. They should know to wear a hat, mittens or gloves, and layered clothing appropriate for the climate and conditions.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a winter sports day (Beaveree, Cuboree, winter challenge).
requirements.requirement: Scouts wear clothing made of synthetic fibres to wick moisture and keep dry and warm, or they wear wool clothing, which will stay warm when wet.

requirements.competency: I know how to dress when I go outside in winter.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the buddy system and why it is used, providing some examples of winter emergencies.

requirements.competency: I know to stay dry or change to dry clothes when I am outside in winter.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain why they must be especially careful to follow the instructions of an activity leader to make sure that they stay safe from winter and normal hazards.

requirements.competency: I know about the buddy system and why it is used when participating in outdoor activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have taken part in a short winter hike, wearing appropriate clothing and packing appropriate gear. They have used the buddy system.

requirements.competency: I know to follow the instructions of the activity leader when at an outdoor event.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe why they should not touch cold metal with bare skin, including what will happen (skin freezes to metal) and what injuries can result (at worst, some skin remains frozen to the metal, resulting in a serious abrasion).

requirements.competency: I have gone sledding or tobogganing.
requirements.requirement: When sledding or tobogganing, Scouts follow appropriate safety precautions: staying away from roads and other dangerous obstacles, watching out for others on the hill and wearing appropriate clothing and helmets.

requirements.competency: I know to avoid ice or open water without an adult present.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe how to make a nutritious and tasty meal to eat outdoors in the winter. Packed lunches should include as little garbage as possible.

requirements.competency: I have made a piece of simple winter gear or clothing (wristlet or neck warmer).
requirements.requirement: Scouts have participated in two winter sports days, at least one of which was in support of this stage.

requirements.competency: I have packed a proper lunch for a winter outing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe the appropriate clothing for winter outings, including what extra clothing should be packed for a scenario. They can also describe why these clothing articles are appropriate—for example, synthetic fibres will not hold moisture like cotton; wet cotton will make one cold.

requirements.competency: I have participated in two winter sports days (Beaveree, Cuboree, winter challenge).
requirements.requirement: Scouts know how to keep their feet dry by using waterproof boots and staying out of water. Scouts can describe why a change of dry wool or synthetic socks is important.

requirements.competency: I know how to pack extra clothes for winter outings.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe how to hang up clothing to dry safely (not burning, melting or catching fire) and effectively.

requirements.competency: I know how to keep my feet dry while I am outside in the winter.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can help less experienced Scouts with tying boots, doing up zippers or making sure they have their toques and mittens on properly.

requirements.competency: I store my outdoor clothing so it will quickly dry.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can spend one night at a winter camp. At this stage, it is acceptable for the Scout to have spent the night in a heated cabin or other permanent shelter.

requirements.competency: I can help less experienced Scouts to get dressed to go outside.
requirements.requirement: With their Section and/or Patrol, Scouts can complete two short hikes in winter conditions, specifically for this stage.

requirements.competency: I have attended one overnight winter camp or sleepover.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate an understanding of this hazard and know to avoid iced-over rivers, lakes or ponds without adult supervision.

requirements.competency: I have completed two winter hikes of at least 1 km.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can make a piece of winter gear or clothing and describe its use and value for winter Adventures.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a winter sport (alpine skiing, cross-country skiing, snowshoeing, snowboarding, skating, hockey, tobogganing, sledding, curling).
requirements.requirement: With members of their Section, family or through school, Scouts will have participated in a winter sporting activity (such as one listed above). It is not expected that Scouts will participate in a league or achieve a specific proficiency in the sport.

requirements.competency: I have spent one night at winter camp in a cabin or heated tent (in addition to requirements for previous stages).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the ability to light a small fire in winter conditions. The principles of Leave No Trace should be adhered to.

requirements.competency: I can identify the North Star and three other features in the winter night sky.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can work with members of their team to produce a balanced menu for a winter camp.

requirements.competency: I have completed a winter hike of at least 3 km.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can cook a simple meal over an open fire.

requirements.competency: I have made a winter survival kit that I take with me all winter activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain the principles behind layering clothes (wicking, warmth and wind/wet) for winter activities and have an opportunity to demonstrate this skill. Scouts understand what clothing fabrics are appropriate and are aware of less-expensive options.

requirements.competency: In addition to previous stages, I have made a piece of winter gear or clothing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can build a simple emergency shelter with tarp, piece of plastic, snow or other easily-obtainable items and materials. (At this stage it is not expected that Scouts will be able to produce an “expert” shelter; rather, they should have had an opportunity to work as a member of a team trying to build a simple shelter.)

requirements.competency: I can light a small fire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the ability to find shelter from the wind when outdoors in winter conditions.

requirements.competency: I have helped plan a menu for a winter camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the ability to pack a personal day pack for a winter outing.

requirements.competency: I have cooked a lunch over an open fire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to identify signs of hypothermia and/or frostbite. (Please refer to the Field Book for Canadian Scouting.)

requirements.competency: I understand the layering principle when dressing for winter activities and apply it to all activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can spend one night at a winter camp in a cabin or heated tent.

requirements.competency: With a small group, I have built an emergency shelter in winter.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can identify the North Star as well as some of the constellations and/or planets in the winter night sky, and can describe why they can be valuable for navigation and culture.

requirements.competency: I know how to find shelter from the wind a cold day.
requirements.requirement: As part of their Section and/or Patrol, Scouts can complete a 3 km hike in winter conditions.

requirements.competency: I can pack a day pack for a winter outing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can make a winter survival kit that is suitable for all winter activities. Scouts can explain why they have included the items in the kit and how each can be used in a winter emergency, and they can explain why other possible items are not included. Scouts can describe possible scenarios (lost in the woods, stranded in a vehicle or cabin due to the weather) in which the kit could be used.

requirements.competency: I know how to watch my fellow Scouts for signs of exposure to the cold.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can make a significant item of winter gear or clothing.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a winter sport (different from the sport done in earlier stages).
requirements.requirement: With members of their Section, family, or through school, Scouts can participate in a winter sporting activity. (It is not expected that Scouts will participate in a league or achieve a specific level of proficiency in the given sport.)

requirements.competency: I have traveled in snow using snowshoes or nordic skis.
requirements.requirement: With members of their Patrol, Scouts can assist in purchasing appropriate food for a winter outing. They can explain the rationale for the food chosen, addressing considerations such as taste, nutrition, cost and packing weight and volume.

requirements.competency: I have participated in two 3 km hikes.
requirements.requirement: As part of a team, Scouts can help prepare more than one meal for their Patrol while at winter camp.

requirements.competency: I know how to help someone who has fallen through the ice.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can participate in at least one outdoor winter campfire.

requirements.competency: I know how to prevent and treat hypothermia and frostbite.
requirements.requirement: As a part of their Patrol, Scouts can put up a tent in winter conditions and sleep in it overnight.

requirements.competency: I know how to avoid and treat snow blindness.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how to properly set up their sleeping areas to stay warm and comfortable while at winter camp.

requirements.competency: I have helped purchase food for a winter outing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can use a toboggan or sled to transport personal and/or Patrol equipment to camp.

requirements.competency: I have helped cook meals at winter camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can teach a winter skill to a less-experienced Scout.

requirements.competency: I have attended a winter campfire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can spend two consecutive nights at a winter camp. At this stage, it is acceptable for the Scout to spend one of these two nights in a heated cabin or other permanent shelter.

requirements.competency: I have put up a tent in winter to sleep in.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can travel in snow wearing snowshoes or nordic skis.

requirements.competency: I can properly set up my sleeping area to stay warm through the night at winter camp.
requirements.requirement: As part of their Section and/or Patrol, Scouts can complete two 3 km hike in winter conditions.

requirements.competency: I have used a toboggan or sled to transport equipment.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate how assist in rescuing someone who has fallen through the ice. Please refer to the Field Book for Canadian Scouting.

requirements.competency: I have taught a winter skill to a less experienced Scout.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain and demonstrate how to prevent and treat hypothermia and frostbite. Please refer to the Field Book for Canadian Scouting.

requirements.competency: I have spent two consecutive nights at winter camp (in addition to requirements for previous stages).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain and demonstrate how to prevent and treat snow blindness. Please refer to the Field Book for Canadian Scouting.

requirements.competency: I have played an outdoor game in winter at a minimum of six Scout meetings.
requirements.requirement: Outdoor games at a winter Scout meeting can include anything from tag to snowshoe soccer. An associated requirement is that Scouts come to all meetings prepared to spend time outside (regardless of the weather) in order to be able to complete this requirement.

requirements.competency: I have slept outside for two nights in winter (in addition to requirements for previous stages).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can lead their Patrols in planning and purchasing food for a winter camp. Scouts can explain the rationale behind the menu, explaining considerations such as taste, nutrition, budget and food weight and volume.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a winter hike lasting at least six hours and covering at least 6 km.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can lead Patrols in preparing at least one balanced and tasty meal while at winter camp.

requirements.competency: I have hiked a minimum of 5 km in winter conditions following compass bearings.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can assist in running a formal campfire while at a winter camp. This could include laying the fire, running the opening, planning the program, leading some songs or telling a story.

requirements.competency: I know how to avoid, recognize and treat carbon monoxide poisoning (which can occur in winter shelters).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate the ability to pack appropriate clothing and gear for a two-night winter camp.

requirements.competency: I have a first aid qualification equivalent to the Scout First Aid badge (St John’s or Red Cross Standard First Aid).
requirements.requirement: As a part of their Patrol, Scouts can build and sleep in a temporary shelter that they have built themselves.

requirements.competency: I can execute a ladder rescue, chain assist and reaching assist for someone who has fallen through the ice.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can lay, light and maintain a fire in winter conditions that can be used for cooking and for comfort.

requirements.competency: I have led my Patrol in planning and purchasing meals for a winter camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can safely use a liquid fuel stove in winter conditions; they can safely transfer fuel, safely and properly set up a stove on a suitable surface, and light and manage the stove. Scouts can explain safety hazards of using a stove and handling fuel, and how to manage identified hazards. Scouts can explain appropriate responses to possible emergency scenarios related to operating a liquid fuel stove in winter conditions.

requirements.competency: I have been chief cook for at least one meal at winter camp.
requirements.requirement: As a part of a Patrol, Scouts can lead a winter sports day for a younger Section.

requirements.competency: I have helped to run a campfire at a winter camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can spend two consecutive nights at a winter camp. At this stage, it is expected that Scouts stay in a temporary shelter that they have built or a tent.

requirements.competency: I am able to pack the appropriate clothing and equipment for a two-night winter camp.
requirements.requirement: As a part of a Patrol, Scouts can hike for at least six hours, covering at least 6 km. This can be done using snowshoes or nordic skis.

requirements.competency: I have built and slept in a temporary winter shelter such as a quinzhee, snow trench or a lean-to.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can hike a minimum of 5 km following compass bearings. This can be done as a part of the six-hour hike completed for this stage.

requirements.competency: I can lay and light a fire in winter conditions for warmth and cooking.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe how to avoid and treat carbon monoxide poisoning. Scouts can identify possible causes of carbon monoxide poisoning related to winter camping.

requirements.competency: I can use a liquid fuel stove in winter conditions and understand why it is important not to get fuel clothing and skin.
requirements.requirement: Scouts will have completed standard first aid training.

requirements.competency: I have helped lead a winter sports day.
requirements.requirement: Scouts Canada Field Book pages 188–89 describe the required rescue techniques.

requirements.competency: I have led an outdoor winter game for a younger Section.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can select, teach and lead an appropriate outdoor winter game for a younger Section.

requirements.competency: I have slept outside for two nights in a lightweight shelter in winter (in addition to requirements for previous levels).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe how water can be obtained by melting snow or ice, or sourcing from open water or under ice. Scouts can describe how melting snow or ice will impact a camp with regard to time and gear (eg-fuel). Scouts can describe how to determine if ice is safe to venture out on, and how to treat water to make it safe to drink. Scouts can list what gear is needed to source water in winter conditions (axe, auger, safety equipment, purification equipment).

requirements.competency: I have practised a winter evacuation of a simulated casualty.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can describe how to keep water from freezing by keeping it in secure and near one’s body in a sleeping bag.

requirements.competency: I know how to obtain and keep a supply of safe drinking water for a winter camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know how to bake in winter conditions using an improvised trail oven using pots, a commercial trail oven or reflector oven.

requirements.competency: I know how to store water overnight so that it will not freeze.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can teach less experienced Scouts about dressing in layers (wicking, warmth and wind/wet), what clothing fabrics are appropriate and less expensive options.

requirements.competency: I have baked bread or a dessert at a winter camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can teach less experienced Scouts to construct a shelter from snow or other materials (tarp, parachute, etc.).

requirements.competency: I have taught a less experienced Scout how to dress for winter activities.
requirements.requirement: Scouts know how to: • dry out a stove that has snow or water in the burners • oil the pump leather when the pump will not pressurize the fuel tank

requirements.competency: I have taught a less experienced Scout how to build a winter shelter.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can lead a team in cooking all meals (breakfast, lunch and supper) at a camp. Scouts can describe the rationale behind the menu: taste, nutrition, budget and weight and volume considerations.

requirements.competency: I can do simple repairs liquid fuel stoves.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can help at a winter Beaveree, Cuboree or community event where the activities are primarily outside.

requirements.competency: I have led a cooking team for a winter camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can sleep outside in winter conditions in a lightweight shelter such as a tent.

requirements.competency: I have assisted at a winter sports day in a leadership role.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can practise a winter evacuation using a sled or other improvised self-propelled method of person/gear transportation to a road .5 km from the simulated injury.

requirements.competency: I know how to plan and implement a simple but nutritious menu for a mobile winter camp.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can plan a menu that will meet the nutritional needs of growing youth in the cold. The food can be easily transported using packs or on sleds, depending how the Scouts plan to carry gear.

requirements.competency: I know how to select an appropriate tent for winter camping.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can explain what makes a tent appropriate for winter camping (ventilation, snow load, overall stability).

requirements.competency: I have made a piece of winter camping or winter safety equipment.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can make a piece of winter camping or winter safety equipment, such as: snow goggles, ice recovery picks, snowshoes, adapted pulk sled for hauling winter gear, or freight toboggan).

requirements.competency: I have participated in a winter mobile expedition of at least three days (two nights).
requirements.requirement: Scouts can participate in a challenging winter day hike (back country, alpine) appropriate to their region. Scouts can prepare the emergency plan for a winter day hike or overnight camp that takes into consideration local winter dangers such as avalanche, wet climate, wind chill, freezing rain, or other weather patterns that could lead to unsafe conditions for their Section. Scouts can submit the plan to the Group Commissioner for approval with the Outdoor Activity Application.

requirements.competency: I have maintained my first-aid certification.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can maintain standard first-aid certification officially recognized by a reputable organization, such as St. John’s Ambulance, the Red Cross, or a local EMT provider.

requirements.competency: I can carry out repairs gas-fuelled stoves in winter conditions.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can determine the causes of problems and conduct the necessary repairs to keep stoves running at camp in winter conditions.

requirements.competency: With a team, I have provided leadership for a one or two-night activity.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can provide a fun and safe overnight activity in the winter for younger Scouts. Scouts can engage less-experienced youth in the Plan-Do-Review process.

requirements.competency: I have participated in a five-day (four-night) mobile winter expedition.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can participate in a five-day mobile expedition in winter. Scouts can demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of the risks and mitigating measures for the climatic conditions in a specific region during winter and can select the most appropriate equipment for an activity.

requirements.competency: I understand the risks and am able to implement appropriate safety procedures for camping in heated tents.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can demonstrate an understanding of the challenges and advantages of hot tenting and can describe the specific safety considerations in selecting equipment for this activity.

requirements.competency: I have wilderness first aid certifications appropriate for areas I am traveling in and the activities I am doing.
requirements.requirement: Scouts have the necessary qualifications and skills to provide first aid during an extended winter expedition. Scouts can recognize and treat conditions common in winter conditions such as snow blindness, frostbite, hypothermia and carbon monoxide poisoning; furthermore, Scouts know how to prevent such medical conditions.

requirements.competency: I have led a winter campfire.
requirements.requirement: Scouts can lead a winter campfire, and describe the hazards of the activity (especially for younger Scouts) and how they can be addressed.

requirements.competency: I have led a mobile winter expedition of three to five days.
requirements.requirement: As the leader of an extended winter expedition, Scouts can demonstrate the skills required to ensure the safety and comfort of the expedition members (using Plan-Do-Review).

requirements.competency: I have slept outside at least 20 nights (including nights from previous levels) in winter conditions.
requirements.requirement: Through the course of all stages, Scouts have slept in cabins, tents and temporary shelters for at least 20 nights.
